languages.name: ANF
languages.definition: A Boolean formula represented exclusively using the exclusive-OR (XOR) and AND logical operators, taking the shape of a XOR sum of conjunctions. Originally introduced as Zhegalkin polynomials, it is highly structured but generally less space-efficient for standard compilation tasks compared to decision graphs \citet{Zhegalkin_1927}.

languages.name: BDD
languages.definition: A directed acyclic graph where each internal node represents a true/false decision on a specific boolean variable. Paths from the root to the leaf nodes explicitly map sequential variable assignments to a final 0 or 1 output \citet{Lee_1959}.

languages.name: CNF
languages.definition: A standard logical representation where a formula is an AND of clauses, with each clause being an OR of literals. While it is the foundational input format for modern SAT solvers, it supports very few polynomial-time operations natively \citet{Boole_1847}.

CO.description: Consistency checking for \langref{CNF} is NP-complete (the classic SAT problem). This is one of the foundational intractability results (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 4.1, Table 16).

IM.description: To check whether a consistent term $\alpha$ implies a \langref{CNF} sentence $\Sigma$, it suffices to verify that each clause of $\Sigma$ shares a literal with $\alpha$. This can be done in polynomial time (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 4.1, Table 21).

VA.description: A \langref{CNF} sentence is valid iff every clause in it is valid (i.e., contains a complementary pair of literals). This can be checked in linear time (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 4.1, Table 21).

CT.description: \nopref{lang_27fffab2}{CT} (unless the polynomial hierarchy collapses) \citet{Darwiche_2002,Roth_1996}, and \edgeref{lang_27fffab2}{lang_89649e36} in polynomial time. If CNF supported Model Counting in polynomial time, then PI could too by compiling first. Therefore Model Counting is unsupported by CNF (unless the polynomial hierarchy collapses).

EQ.description: Since Equivalence implies Consistency \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_89649e36}{CO} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, then Equivalence is unsupported by CNF as well (unless P = NP).

SE.description: Since Sentential Entailment implies Equivalence \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_89649e36}{EQ} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, then Sentential Entailment is unsupported by CNF as well (unless P = NP).

CE.description: Since Clausal Entailment implies Consistency \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_89649e36}{CO} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, then Clausal Entailment is unsupported by CNF as well (unless P = NP).

ME.description: Since Model Enumeration implies Consistency \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_89649e36}{CO} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, then Model Enumeration is unsupported by CNF as well (unless P = NP).

AND_C.description: Conjunction of CNF sentences is trivially another CNF sentence: simply take the union of all clauses (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

OR_BC.description: The disjunction of two CNF sentences $\alpha_1 \lor \alpha_2$ can be computed by taking all cross products of one clause from $\alpha_1$ and one from $\alpha_2$, removing redundant literals and valid clauses. The result is a CNF of polynomial size (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

OR_C.description: It is straightforward to convert any term into an equivalent CNF formula in polynomial time. Since specific DNF formulas cannot be turned into equivalent CNF formulas in polynomial space (DNF $\not\leq$ CNF), CNF does not satisfy $\lor$C (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

CD.description: CD is trivially satisfied: replacing literals of the conditioning term by Boolean constants in a CNF and removing valid clauses preserves the CNF property (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

FO.description: If CNF satisfies FO, then the consistency of any CNF sentence $\Sigma$ could be tested in polytime: compute $\exists\text{Vars}(\Sigma).\Sigma$ and check validity. Since CNF does not satisfy CO, this implies P = NP (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

AND_BC.description: Since \langref{lang_89649e36} supports Conjunction, it also supports Bounded Conjunction \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

SFO.description: Since \langref{lang_89649e36} supports Conditioning, Bounded Disjunction, it also supports Singleton Forgetting \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

NOT_C.description: Since Negation ∧ Conjunction implies Disjunction \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_89649e36}{OR_C} \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since Conjunction is supported in polynomial time, then Negation is unsupported by CNF as well.

languages.name: cSDD

CE.description: \edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{CE} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2001a}. Therefore cSDD supports Clausal Entailment in polynomial time.

CT.description: \edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6c130090}{CT} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2001b}. Therefore cSDD supports Model Counting in polynomial time.

CO.description: Since \langref{lang_83e3b023} supports Model Counting, it also supports Consistency \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

VA.description: Since \langref{lang_83e3b023} supports Model Counting, it also supports Validity \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

IM.description: \edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_1afefbe2} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_1afefbe2}{IM} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. Therefore cSDD supports Implicant in polynomial time.

ME.description: \edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6c130090}{ME} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. Therefore cSDD supports Model Enumeration in polynomial time.

SE.description: \edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_ea9b5299}{SE} in polynomial time. Therefore cSDD supports Sentential Entailment in polynomial time.

EQ.description: Since \langref{lang_83e3b023} supports Sentential Entailment, it also supports Equivalence \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

languages.definition:
There are two definitions for a cSDD ("canonical SDD").
First, we say an SDD is \emph{compressed} if no two primes share equivalent subs, \emph{normalized} if each partition follows the current vtree split, and \emph{trimmed} if it has trivial true/false wrapper partitions removed.
Under a fixed vtree, either compressed+normalized or compressed+trimmed entail canonicicity. Since normalization and trimming are efficient, all results apply to either definition. \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015,Darwiche_2011}.

languages.name: d-DNNF

CT.description: \langref{d-DNNF} satisfies CT: model counting can be performed in polynomial time by exploiting decomposability (multiplying counts at $\land$-nodes) and determinism (adding counts at $\lor$-nodes). This is the key tractability result motivating \langref{d-DNNF} as a compilation target \citet{Darwiche_2001b} (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 4.1, Table 26).

CE.description: \edgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{CE} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2001a}. Therefore d-DNNF supports Clausal Entailment in polynomial time.

CO.description: Since \langref{lang_6c130090} supports Model Counting, it also supports Consistency \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

VA.description: Since \langref{lang_6c130090} supports Model Counting, it also supports Validity \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

ME.description: Since \langref{lang_6c130090} supports Consistency, Conditioning, it also supports Model Enumeration \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

IM.description: Since \langref{lang_6c130090} supports Validity, Conditioning, it also supports Implicant \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

EQ.description: \citet{Darwiche_Huang_2002} showed that equivalence of d-DNNF sentences can be tested in randomized polynomial time by reducing it to the problem of checking whether two d-DNNFs agree on a randomly chosen assignment. Under the derandomization hypothesis P = BPP, this yields a deterministic polynomial-time algorithm and hence \langref{d-DNNF} satisfies EQ.

AND_BC.description: Since OBDD $\subseteq$ d-DNNF and d-DNNF satisfies CO, the NP-completeness of consistency checking for two OBDDs (which may possess different underlying orderings) (Lemma 8.14, \citet{Meinel_Theobald_1998}) implies d-DNNF does not satisfy $\land$BC unless P = NP (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

CD.description: Conditioning preserves both decomposability and determinism. If $\alpha \land \beta \models \bot$ then $(\alpha|\gamma) \land (\beta|\gamma) \models \bot$ for any consistent term $\gamma$, since $((\alpha \land \beta)|\gamma) \land \gamma \models \bot$ and $\gamma$ is consistent (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

SFO.description: If d-DNNF satisfies SFO, then it satisfies $\lor$BC (since $\alpha_1 \lor \alpha_2 \equiv \exists x . ((x \land \alpha_1) \lor (\neg x \land \alpha_2))$ for fresh $x$). But d-DNNF does not satisfy $\lor$BC unless P = NP (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

AND_C.description: Since Conjunction implies Bounded Conjunction \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_6c130090}{AND_BC} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, then Conjunction is unsupported by d-DNNF as well (unless P = NP).

OR_BC.description: Since Conditioning ∧ Bounded Disjunction implies Singleton Forgetting \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_6c130090}{SFO} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since Conditioning is supported in polynomial time, then Bounded Disjunction is unsupported by d-DNNF as well (unless P = NP).

OR_C.description: Since Disjunction implies Bounded Disjunction \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_6c130090}{OR_BC} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, then Disjunction is unsupported by d-DNNF as well (unless P = NP).

FO.description: Since Forgetting implies Singleton Forgetting \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_6c130090}{SFO} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, then Forgetting is unsupported by d-DNNF as well (unless P = NP).

languages.definition: A variant of DNNF where the children of every OR node represent mutually exclusive (deterministic) formulas. This added determinism is the critical property that enables efficient, linear-time model counting and probabilistic inference \citet{Darwiche_2001a}.

languages.name: d-SDNNF

CE.description: \edgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{CE} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2001a}. Therefore d-SDNNF supports Clausal Entailment in polynomial time.

CT.description: \edgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6c130090}{CT} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2001b}. Therefore d-SDNNF supports Model Counting in polynomial time.

CO.description: Since \langref{lang_ea9b5299} supports Model Counting, it also supports Consistency \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

VA.description: Since \langref{lang_ea9b5299} supports Model Counting, it also supports Validity \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

IM.description: Since \langref{lang_ea9b5299} supports Validity, Conditioning, it also supports Implicant \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

ME.description: Since \langref{lang_ea9b5299} supports Consistency, Conditioning, it also supports Model Enumeration \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

SE.description: Since \langref{lang_ea9b5299} supports Model Counting, Bounded Conjunction, it also supports Sentential Entailment.

EQ.description: Since \langref{lang_ea9b5299} supports Sentential Entailment, it also supports Equivalence \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

NOT_C.description: Structured deterministic DNNF (\langref{d-SDNNF}) is not closed under negation in polytime. \citet{Vinall-Smeeth_2024} proves that for every $n \in \mathbb{N}$, there exists a Boolean function $f$ with an equivalent structured $d$-DNNF of size $n$ such that any structured DNNF equivalent to $\neg f$ has size $n^{\Omega(\tilde{\log}\, n)}$. This super-polynomial lower bound holds for every vtree (not just a single tree), so the result applies to \langref{d-SDNNF} (not just \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}). Whether quasi-polynomial closure holds remains open.

AND_BC.description: Bounded conjunction of two \langref{d-SDNNF} formulas sharing the same vtree can be performed in polytime via the Apply algorithm, preserving both determinism and structured decomposability (\citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}, Proposition 4). Note: requires formulas to follow the same vtree.

CD.description: Conditioning preserves both structured decomposability and determinism. The vtree structure is maintained since $\text{Vars}(\Sigma|\gamma) \subseteq \text{Vars}(\Sigma)$, and mutual exclusivity of $\lor$-children is preserved under conditioning (\citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}, Proposition 4).

FO.description: If \langref{d-SDNNF} satisfied FO, then every \langref{DNF} could be compiled to an equivalent \langref{d-SDNNF} of polynomial size (using fresh selector variables and applying FO). Since specific \langref{DNF}s require exponential-size \langref{d-SDNNF} representations (unconditionally), this is impossible. This unconditional result applies to the fixed-vtree case (\citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}, Proposition 4).

AND_C.description: Any clause can be trivially expressed as a \langref{d-SDNNF} formula. If \langref{d-SDNNF} satisfied $\land$C, any \langref{CNF} could be compiled to \langref{d-SDNNF} in polytime. Since specific \langref{CNF}s require exponential-size \langref{d-SDNNF} representations (unconditionally), this is impossible (\citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}, Proposition 4).

OR_C.description: Any term can be trivially expressed as a \langref{d-SDNNF} formula. If \langref{d-SDNNF} satisfied $\lor$C, any \langref{DNF} could be compiled to \langref{d-SDNNF} in polytime. Since specific \langref{DNF}s require exponential-size \langref{d-SDNNF} representations (unconditionally), this is impossible (\citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}, Proposition 4).

languages.definition: A deterministic DNNF where the variable decomposition across the graph is governed by a hierarchical variable tree (vtree). \citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}.

languages.name: d-SDNNF$_T$

CE.description: \edgeref{lang_91f812d0}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{CE} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2001a}. Therefore d-SDNNF$_T$ supports Clausal Entailment in polynomial time.

EQ.description: Since \langref{lang_91f812d0} supports Sentential Entailment, it also supports Equivalence \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

CT.description: \edgeref{lang_91f812d0}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6c130090}{CT} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2001b}. Therefore d-SDNNF$_T$ supports Model Counting in polynomial time.

CO.description: Since \langref{lang_91f812d0} supports Model Counting, it also supports Consistency \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

VA.description: Since \langref{lang_91f812d0} supports Model Counting, it also supports Validity \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

SE.description: \edgeref{lang_91f812d0}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_ea9b5299}{SE} in polynomial time. Therefore d-SDNNF$_T$ supports Sentential Entailment in polynomial time.

IM.description: \edgeref{lang_91f812d0}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6c130090}{IM} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. Therefore d-SDNNF$_T$ supports Implicant in polynomial time.

ME.description: \edgeref{lang_91f812d0}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6c130090}{ME} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. Therefore d-SDNNF$_T$ supports Model Enumeration in polynomial time.

languages.definition: The set of all d-SDNNF formulas that follow a fixed vtree $T$ \citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}.

languages.name: DNF
languages.definition: A logical formula expressed as an OR of terms, where each term is an AND of literals.

VA.description: Validity checking for \langref{DNF} is coNP-complete. A \langref{DNF} is valid iff every assignment satisfies at least one term, which requires checking that term coverage is complete (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 4.1, Table 16).

CE.description: \edgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{CE} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2001a}. Therefore DNF supports Clausal Entailment in polynomial time.

CO.description: Since \langref{lang_4c204bf3} supports Clausal Entailment, it also supports Consistency \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

ME.description: Since \langref{lang_4c204bf3} supports Consistency, Conditioning, it also supports Model Enumeration \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

CT.description: \nopref{lang_6ae90adc}{CT} (unless the polynomial hierarchy collapses) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and \edgeref{lang_6ae90adc}{lang_4c204bf3} in polynomial time. If DNF supported Model Counting in polynomial time, then IP could too by compiling first. Therefore Model Counting is unsupported by DNF (unless the polynomial hierarchy collapses).

EQ.description: Since Equivalence implies Validity \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_4c204bf3}{VA} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, then Equivalence is unsupported by DNF as well (unless P = NP).

SE.description: Since Sentential Entailment implies Equivalence \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_4c204bf3}{EQ} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, then Sentential Entailment is unsupported by DNF as well (unless P = NP).

IM.description: Since Implicant implies Validity \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_4c204bf3}{VA} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, then Implicant is unsupported by DNF as well (unless P = NP).

NOT_C.description: Since Negation ∧ Disjunction implies Conjunction \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_4c204bf3}{AND_C} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since Disjunction is supported in quasi-polynomial time, then Negation is unsupported by DNF in quasi-polynomial time as well.

AND_BC.description: The conjunction of two DNF sentences $\alpha_1 \land \alpha_2$ can be computed by taking all cross products of one term from $\alpha_1$ and one from $\alpha_2$, removing redundant literals and inconsistent terms. The result is a DNF of polynomial size (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

AND_C.description: It is straightforward to convert a clause into an equivalent DNF formula in polynomial time. Since specific CNF formulas cannot be turned into equivalent DNF formulas in quasi-polynomial space (CNF $\not\leq$ DNF), DNF does not satisfy $\land$C (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

OR_C.description: Disjunction of DNF sentences is trivially another DNF sentence: simply take the union of all terms (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

FO.description: DNF satisfies FO \citet{Lang_2003}. Forgetting variables from a DNF sentence can be done in polytime (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

OR_BC.description: Since \langref{lang_4c204bf3} supports Disjunction, it also supports Bounded Disjunction \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

SFO.description: Since \langref{lang_4c204bf3} supports Forgetting, it also supports Singleton Forgetting \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

CD.description: Since \langref{lang_4c204bf3} supports Bounded Conjunction, Singleton Forgetting, it also supports Conditioning \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

languages.name: DNNF

CE.description: \langref{DNNF} supports clause entailment (CE) in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2001a}. To test $\Sigma \models \gamma$ for clause $\gamma$, it suffices to test whether $\Sigma | \neg\gamma$ is inconsistent. Since \langref{DNNF} satisfies CD and CO, this can be done in polytime.

CO.description: Since \langref{lang_3bebcab7} supports Clausal Entailment, it also supports Consistency \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

ME.description: Since \langref{lang_3bebcab7} supports Consistency, Conditioning, it also supports Model Enumeration \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

VA.description: \nopref{lang_4c204bf3}{VA} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and \edgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time. If DNNF supported Validity in polynomial time, then DNF could too by compiling first. Therefore Validity is unsupported by DNNF (unless P = NP).

SE.description: Since Sentential Entailment implies Equivalence \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_3bebcab7}{EQ} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, then Sentential Entailment is unsupported by DNNF as well (unless P = NP).

CT.description: \nopref{lang_6ae90adc}{CT} (unless the polynomial hierarchy collapses) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and \edgeref{lang_6ae90adc}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time. If DNNF supported Model Counting in polynomial time, then IP could too by compiling first. Therefore Model Counting is unsupported by DNNF (unless the polynomial hierarchy collapses).

EQ.description: Since Equivalence implies Validity \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_3bebcab7}{VA} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, then Equivalence is unsupported by DNNF as well (unless P = NP).

IM.description: Since Implicant implies Validity \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_3bebcab7}{VA} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, then Implicant is unsupported by DNNF as well (unless P = NP).

AND_BC.description: Checking consistency of two OBDDs (which may possess different underlying variable orderings) is NP-complete (Lemma 8.14, \citet{Meinel_Theobald_1998}). Since OBDD $\subseteq$ DNNF and DNNF satisfies CO, if DNNF satisfied $\land$BC then consistency of two OBDDs could be tested in polytime, implying P = NP (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

OR_C.description: Disjunction trivially preserves decomposability since decomposability only constrains $\land$-nodes. (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

CD.description: Conditioning preserves decomposability: if $\alpha$ and $\beta$ do not share variables, then $\alpha|\gamma$ and $\beta|\gamma$ do not share variables either, since $\text{Vars}(\alpha|\gamma) \subseteq \text{Vars}(\alpha)$ (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

FO.description: DNNF satisfies FO: forgetting a set of variables in a DNNF sentence can be performed in polynomial time while preserving decomposability \citet{Darwiche_2001a} (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

OR_BC.description: Since \langref{lang_3bebcab7} supports Disjunction, it also supports Bounded Disjunction \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

SFO.description: Since \langref{lang_3bebcab7} supports Forgetting, it also supports Singleton Forgetting \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

AND_C.description: Since Conjunction implies Bounded Conjunction \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_3bebcab7}{AND_BC} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, then Conjunction is unsupported by DNNF as well (unless P = NP).

NOT_C.description: Since Negation ∧ Disjunction implies Conjunction \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_3bebcab7}{AND_C} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since Disjunction is supported in polynomial time, then Negation is unsupported by DNNF as well (unless P = NP).

languages.definition: The subset of NNF where the children of every AND node operate on completely disjoint sets of variables. This non-overlapping variable property, known as decomposability, guarantees polynomial-time consistency checking \citet{Darwiche_2001a}.

languages.name: FBDD

CO.description: \langref{FBDD} satisfies CO \citet{Gergov_1994, Darwiche_2002}. Consistency is polynomial: check if the BDD root is not the terminal 0 node.

CT.description: \langref{FBDD} satisfies CT \citet{Gergov_1994, Darwiche_2002}. Model counting on FBDDs can be done in polynomial time by a single bottom-up pass counting satisfying assignments at each node.

VA.description: \langref{FBDD} satisfies VA \citet{Gergov_1994, Darwiche_2002}. Simply check if the BDD root equals the terminal 1 node.

CE.description: \edgeref{lang_684b1ca7}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{CE} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2001a}. Therefore FBDD supports Clausal Entailment in polynomial time.

ME.description: Since \langref{lang_684b1ca7} supports Consistency, Conditioning, it also supports Model Enumeration \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

IM.description: Since \langref{lang_684b1ca7} supports Validity, Conditioning, it also supports Implicant \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

EQ.description: \citet{Blum_1980} showed that equivalence of free Boolean graphs (FBDDs) can be decided in randomized polynomial time (BPP). Under the derandomization hypothesis P = BPP, this gives polynomial-time equivalence testing for \langref{FBDD}.

NOT_C.description: Negation of an FBDD is achieved by switching the labels of the 0-sink and 1-sink. The decision property and read-once property are preserved (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

CD.description: Conditioning on FBDD is the restrict operation: replace each node labeled by a variable in the conditioning term with one of its children. This preserves the free (read-once) property (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

SFO.description: If FBDD satisfies SFO, then it satisfies $\lor$BC (since disjunction can be encoded via SFO with a fresh variable). But FBDD does not satisfy $\lor$BC unless P = NP (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

FO.description: Since Forgetting implies Singleton Forgetting \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_684b1ca7}{SFO} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, then Forgetting is unsupported by FBDD as well (unless P = NP).

OR_BC.description: Since Conditioning ∧ Bounded Disjunction implies Singleton Forgetting \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_684b1ca7}{SFO} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since Conditioning is supported in polynomial time, then Bounded Disjunction is unsupported by FBDD as well (unless P = NP).

AND_BC.description: Since Negation ∧ Bounded Conjunction implies Bounded Disjunction \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_684b1ca7}{OR_BC} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since Negation is supported in polynomial time, then Bounded Conjunction is unsupported by FBDD as well (unless P = NP).

AND_C.description: Since Conjunction implies Bounded Conjunction \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_684b1ca7}{AND_BC} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, then Conjunction is unsupported by FBDD as well (unless P = NP).

OR_C.description: Since Disjunction implies Bounded Disjunction \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_684b1ca7}{OR_BC} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, then Disjunction is unsupported by FBDD as well (unless P = NP).

languages.definition: A Binary Decision Diagram that allows variables to be tested in any order along any given path, provided no variable is tested more than once per path. This property is called the read-once property \citet{Gergov_1994}.

languages.name: IP

CT.description: By duality with \langref{PI}: the negation of a formula in prime implicate form is in prime implicant (\langref{IP}) form. The number of models of $\neg\Sigma$ over $\text{Vars}(\Sigma)$ is $2^n$ minus the model count of $\Sigma$. Hence the \#P-hardness of counting for \langref{PI} transfers to \langref{IP} (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 4.1, Table 24).

IM.description: By definition, \langref{IP} is the set of prime implicants of a formula. A consistent term $\alpha$ implies $\Sigma$ iff $\alpha$ entails some prime implicant of $\Sigma$. This entailment check between terms runs in polynomial time (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 4.1, Table 24).

SE.description: $IP \subseteq DNF$ and \langref{IP} satisfies IM, so checking sentential entailment reduces to checking implication between prime implicant forms. Two formulas are equivalent iff they share the same prime implicants (canonical form), giving SE (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 4.1, Table 24).

CE.description: \edgeref{lang_6ae90adc}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{CE} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2001a}. Therefore IP supports Clausal Entailment in polynomial time.

EQ.description: Since \langref{lang_6ae90adc} supports Sentential Entailment, it also supports Equivalence \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

VA.description: Since \langref{lang_6ae90adc} supports Implicant, it also supports Validity \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

CO.description: Since \langref{lang_6ae90adc} supports Clausal Entailment, it also supports Consistency \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

ME.description: Since \langref{lang_6ae90adc} supports Consistency, Conditioning, it also supports Model Enumeration \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

AND_BC.description: $\text{IP}(\alpha_1 \land \alpha_2) = \max(\{\beta_1 \land \beta_2 \mid \beta_1 \in \text{IP}(\alpha_1), \beta_2 \in \text{IP}(\alpha_2)\}, \models)$ (dual of Proposition 40, Marquis 2000). The maximization removes subsuming terms, yielding an IP form in polytime (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

AND_C.description: It is straightforward to convert a clause into an equivalent IP formula in polynomial time. Since specific CNF formulas cannot be turned into equivalent IP formulas in polynomial space (CNF $\not\leq$ IP), IP does not satisfy $\land$C (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

OR_C.description: Since Disjunction implies Bounded Disjunction \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_6ae90adc}{OR_BC} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}, then Disjunction is unsupported by IP in quasi-polynomial time as well.

CD.description: Given IP formula $\Sigma = \bigvee \gamma_i$, conditioning on $\gamma$ gives $\bigvee (\gamma_i|\gamma)$. Keeping only the logically weakest surviving terms yields a prime implicants formula equivalent to $\Sigma|\gamma$. This requires $O(n^2)$ entailment tests among terms, each polytime (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

SFO.description: The number of prime implicants of $\exists x.\Sigma$ can be exponentially greater than the number of prime implicants of $\Sigma$ \citet{Chandra_1978}. A specific construction with $mk+1$ prime implicants produces $(m+1)^k + mk$ prime implicants after forgetting one variable (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

FO.description: Since Forgetting implies Singleton Forgetting \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_6ae90adc}{SFO} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}, then Forgetting is unsupported by IP in quasi-polynomial time as well.

OR_BC.description: Combining prime implicants via disjunction requires distributing and re-minimizing terms, which can lead to an exponential explosion of new prime implicants. Let $\alpha_1 = \bigwedge_{i=1}^k p_i$ (one prime implicant) and $\alpha_2 = \bigvee_{i=1}^k \bigvee_{j=1}^m (\neg p_i \land q_{i,j})$ ($mk$ prime implicants). Then $\alpha_1 \lor \alpha_2$ has $(m+1)^k + mk$ prime implicants, an exponential blowup \citet{Chandra_1978} (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

NOT_C.description: Dual of PI: $\Sigma_n = \bigvee_{i=0}^{n-1}(x_{2i} \land x_{2i+1})$ is in IP form but has $2^n$ prime implicates. Its negation has $2^n$ prime implicants. Hence IP cannot satisfy $\neg$C (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

languages.definition: A formula represented exactly as the disjunction of all its minimal entailing conjunctions (prime implicants). \citet{Quine_1952}.

languages.name: MODS

CO.description: A sentence $\Sigma$ is consistent iff it has at least one model. Given the set of models, we need only check non-emptyness of the set (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 4.1, Table 18).

CT.description: We need only take the cardinality of the set of models (which can be done in linear time) (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 4.1, Table 18).

VA.description: \edgeref{lang_e02902d0}{lang_89649e36} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_89649e36}{VA} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. Therefore MODS supports Validity in polynomial time.

CE.description: \edgeref{lang_e02902d0}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{CE} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2001a}. Therefore MODS supports Clausal Entailment in polynomial time.

IM.description: \edgeref{lang_e02902d0}{lang_89649e36} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_89649e36}{IM} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. Therefore MODS supports Implicant in polynomial time.

EQ.description: \edgeref{lang_e02902d0}{lang_d69995dd} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_d69995dd}{EQ} in polynomial time \citet{Bryant_1986,Bryant_1992,Meinel_Theobald_1998}. Therefore MODS supports Equivalence in polynomial time.

SE.description: \edgeref{lang_e02902d0}{lang_6ae90adc} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6ae90adc}{SE} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. Therefore MODS supports Sentential Entailment in polynomial time.

ME.description: \edgeref{lang_e02902d0}{lang_d69995dd} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_d69995dd}{ME} in polynomial time \citet{Bryant_1986}. Therefore MODS supports Model Enumeration in polynomial time.

NOT_C.description: $\Sigma = \bigwedge_{i=1}^n x_i$ has only one model over $\{x_1,\ldots,x_n\}$ but its negation $\neg\Sigma$ has $2^n - 1$ models. Hence MODS cannot satisfy $\neg$C (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

AND_BC.description: The bounded conjunction of two MODS formulas (over the same variables) is simply taking the intersection of their model sets. If the variables differ, it is the intersection of their cylindrical extensions. The result is a MODS sentence in polytime (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

AND_C.description: Let $\Sigma_i = (x_{i,1} \lor x_{i,2})$ for $i = 1,\ldots,n$. Each $\Sigma_i$ has 3 models. But $\Sigma = \bigwedge_{i=1}^n \Sigma_i$ has $3^n$ models, so no polynomial-size MODS representation exists (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

FO.description: Given a MODS formula $\Sigma$ and a set $X$, the MODS representation of $\exists X.\Sigma$ is obtained by removing every leaf node labeled by a literal $x$ or $\neg x$ with $x \in X$ (Propositions 18 and 20, Lang et al. 2000). See also the polytime FO operation on DNNF from \citet{Darwiche_2001a} (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

SFO.description: Since \langref{lang_e02902d0} supports Forgetting, it also supports Singleton Forgetting \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

CD.description: Since \langref{lang_e02902d0} supports Bounded Conjunction, Singleton Forgetting, it also supports Conditioning \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

OR_BC.description: Let $\alpha_1 = \bigwedge_{i=1}^n x_i$ (1 model) and $\alpha_2 = y$ (1 model). Then $\alpha_1 \lor \alpha_2$ has $2^n + 1$ models over $\text{Vars}(\alpha_1) \cup \text{Vars}(\alpha_2)$. Since this already uses the disjunction of just two MODS sentences, MODS does not satisfy $\lor$BC (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

OR_C.description: Since Disjunction implies Bounded Disjunction \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_e02902d0}{OR_BC} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}, then Disjunction is unsupported by MODS in quasi-polynomial time as well.

languages.definition: The set of satisfying truth assignments for a given formula. It may be interpreted as the disjunction of each model, and thus as a d-SDNNF formula \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

languages.name: nFBDD

CE.description: \edgeref{lang_1df07cc3}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{CE} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2001a}. Therefore nFBDD supports Clausal Entailment in polynomial time.

CO.description: Since \langref{lang_1df07cc3} supports Clausal Entailment, it also supports Consistency \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

ME.description: \edgeref{lang_1df07cc3}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{ME} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. Therefore nFBDD supports Model Enumeration in polynomial time.

SE.description: Since Sentential Entailment implies Equivalence \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_1df07cc3}{EQ} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, then Sentential Entailment is unsupported by nFBDD as well (unless P = NP).

VA.description: \nopref{lang_4c204bf3}{VA} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and \edgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time. If nFBDD supported Validity in polynomial time, then DNF could too by compiling first. Therefore Validity is unsupported by nFBDD (unless P = NP).

EQ.description: Since Equivalence implies Validity \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_1df07cc3}{VA} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, then Equivalence is unsupported by nFBDD as well (unless P = NP).

IM.description: Since Implicant implies Validity \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_1df07cc3}{VA} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, then Implicant is unsupported by nFBDD as well (unless P = NP).

CT.description: \nopref{lang_6ae90adc}{CT} (unless the polynomial hierarchy collapses) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and \edgeref{lang_6ae90adc}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time. If nFBDD supported Model Counting in polynomial time, then IP could too by compiling first. Therefore Model Counting is unsupported by nFBDD (unless the polynomial hierarchy collapses).

NOT_C.description: Since Negation ∧ Consistency implies Validity \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_1df07cc3}{VA} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since Consistency is supported in polynomial time, then Negation is unsupported by nFBDD as well (unless P = NP).

languages.definition: A generalization of the FBDD that allows for nondeterministic "guess" nodes in addition to standard variable decisions. \citet{Wegener_2000}.

languages.name: NNF

CO.description: \nopref{lang_89649e36}{CO} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and \edgeref{lang_89649e36}{lang_5bf00851} in polynomial time. If NNF supported Consistency in polynomial time, then CNF could too by compiling first. Therefore Consistency is unsupported by NNF (unless P = NP).

VA.description: \nopref{lang_4c204bf3}{VA} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and \edgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_5bf00851} in polynomial time. If NNF supported Validity in polynomial time, then DNF could too by compiling first. Therefore Validity is unsupported by NNF (unless P = NP).

SE.description: Since Sentential Entailment implies Equivalence \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_5bf00851}{EQ} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, then Sentential Entailment is unsupported by NNF as well (unless P = NP).

CT.description: \nopref{lang_6ae90adc}{CT} (unless the polynomial hierarchy collapses) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and \edgeref{lang_6ae90adc}{lang_5bf00851} in polynomial time. If NNF supported Model Counting in polynomial time, then IP could too by compiling first. Therefore Model Counting is unsupported by NNF (unless the polynomial hierarchy collapses).

EQ.description: Since Equivalence implies Consistency \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_5bf00851}{CO} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, then Equivalence is unsupported by NNF as well (unless P = NP).

IM.description: Since Implicant implies Validity \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_5bf00851}{VA} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, then Implicant is unsupported by NNF as well (unless P = NP).

CE.description: Since Clausal Entailment implies Consistency \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_5bf00851}{CO} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, then Clausal Entailment is unsupported by NNF as well (unless P = NP).

ME.description: Since Model Enumeration implies Consistency \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_5bf00851}{CO} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, then Model Enumeration is unsupported by NNF as well (unless P = NP).

NOT_C.description: NNF trivially satisfies $\neg$C since any propositional sentence can be converted to NNF by pushing negations to literals and this preserves the NNF property (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

AND_C.description: Taking the conjunction of NNF sentences $\alpha_1 \land \alpha_2 \land \ldots$ trivially produces another NNF sentence, since NNF places no restrictions on $\land$-nodes (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

CD.description: CD is trivially satisfied by NNF: replacing literals of the conditioning term by Boolean constants preserves the NNF property (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

FO.description: If NNF satisfies FO, then every CNF sentence $\Sigma$ could be tested for consistency in polytime: convert to NNF (trivial), compute $\exists \text{Vars}(\Sigma).\Sigma$ in polytime, and check if the result is valid. Since CNF consistency is NP-hard, NNF does not satisfy FO unless P = NP (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

AND_BC.description: Since \langref{lang_5bf00851} supports Conjunction, it also supports Bounded Conjunction \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

OR_BC.description: Since \langref{lang_5bf00851} supports Negation, Bounded Conjunction, it also supports Bounded Disjunction \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

SFO.description: Since \langref{lang_5bf00851} supports Conditioning, Bounded Disjunction, it also supports Singleton Forgetting \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

OR_C.description: Since \langref{lang_5bf00851} supports Negation, Conjunction, it also supports Disjunction \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

languages.definition: A boolean formula constructed strictly with AND, OR, and literals, where negations are only allowed directly on the variables. It serves as the broad, foundational structural superset for most tractable knowledge compilation languages \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

languages.name: nOBDD

CE.description: \edgeref{lang_d24efe0e}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{CE} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2001a}. Therefore nOBDD supports Clausal Entailment in polynomial time.

CO.description: Since \langref{lang_d24efe0e} supports Clausal Entailment, it also supports Consistency \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

ME.description: \edgeref{lang_d24efe0e}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{ME} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. Therefore nOBDD supports Model Enumeration in polynomial time.

SE.description: Since Sentential Entailment implies Equivalence \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_d24efe0e}{EQ} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, then Sentential Entailment is unsupported by nOBDD as well (unless P = NP).

VA.description: \nopref{lang_4c204bf3}{VA} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and \edgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_d24efe0e} in polynomial time. If nOBDD supported Validity in polynomial time, then DNF could too by compiling first. Therefore Validity is unsupported by nOBDD (unless P = NP).

CT.description: \nopref{lang_6ae90adc}{CT} (unless the polynomial hierarchy collapses) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and \edgeref{lang_6ae90adc}{lang_d24efe0e} in polynomial time. If nOBDD supported Model Counting in polynomial time, then IP could too by compiling first. Therefore Model Counting is unsupported by nOBDD (unless the polynomial hierarchy collapses).

EQ.description: Since Equivalence implies Validity \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_d24efe0e}{VA} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, then Equivalence is unsupported by nOBDD as well (unless P = NP).

IM.description: Since Implicant implies Validity \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_d24efe0e}{VA} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, then Implicant is unsupported by nOBDD as well (unless P = NP).

NOT_C.description: Since Negation ∧ Consistency implies Validity \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_d24efe0e}{VA} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since Consistency is supported in polynomial time, then Negation is unsupported by nOBDD as well (unless P = NP).

languages.definition: A generalization of the OBDD that permits nondeterministic branching while still enforcing a global linear variable ordering for its standard decision nodes. \citet{Wegener_2000}.

languages.fullName: Ordered Binary Decision Diagram (wrt a fixed variable order)
languages.name: OBDD$_<$

CO.description: Consistency is trivial for OBDDs: check if the diagram root is not the terminal 0 node \citet{Bryant_1986}.

CT.description: Model counting on OBDDs can be done in polynomial time by a single bottom-up pass \citet{Bryant_1986, Bryant_1992}.

EQ.description: OBDDs with a fixed variable ordering are canonical: two functions are equivalent iff their reduced OBDDs are identical. This makes EQ trivial (structural isomorphism check) \citet{Bryant_1986, Bryant_1992, Meinel_Theobald_1998} (Theorem 8.11).

ME.description: Models of an OBDD can be enumerated in time polynomial in the output size by traversing all root-to-1-sink paths. Each path corresponds to a (partial) assignment that can be expanded to full models.

VA.description: Validity is trivial for OBDDs: check if the diagram root equals the terminal 1 node \citet{Bryant_1986}.

CE.description: \edgeref{lang_d69995dd}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{CE} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2001a}. Therefore OBDD$_<$ supports Clausal Entailment in polynomial time.

IM.description: Since \langref{lang_d69995dd} supports Validity, Conditioning, it also supports Implicant \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

SE.description: Since \langref{lang_d69995dd} supports Negation, Bounded Conjunction, Consistency, it also supports Sentential Entailment \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

AND_C.description: It is straightforward to convert a clause into an equivalent OBDD$_<$ formula in polynomial time. Since specific CNF formulas cannot be turned into equivalent OBDD$_<$ formulas in polynomial space (CNF $\not\leq$ OBDD$_<$), OBDD$_<$ does not satisfy $\land$C (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

FO.description: If OBDD$_<$ satisfies FO, then every DNF sentence $\Gamma$ can be compiled to an equivalent OBDD$_<$ of polynomial size (using fresh selector variables and then applying FO). Since DNF $\not\leq$ OBDD$_<$, this is impossible (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

NOT_C.description: Negation of an OBDD$_<$ is achieved by switching the labels of the 0-sink and 1-sink. The result is an OBDD$_<$ of identical size \citet{Bryant_1986}.

AND_BC.description: The Apply algorithm computes the conjunction of two OBDD$_<$ sentences sharing the same variable ordering in polynomial time. The result is a reduced OBDD$_<$ \citet{Bryant_1986}.

OR_BC.description: The Apply algorithm computes the disjunction of two OBDD$_<$ sentences sharing the same variable ordering in polynomial time. The result is a reduced OBDD$_<$ \citet{Bryant_1986}.

CD.description: Conditioning (the restrict operation) on OBDD$_<$ replaces each node labeled by a variable in the conditioning term with one of its children, according to the sign of the variable. The result is a valid OBDD$_<$ \citet{Bryant_1986}.

SFO.description: Since \langref{lang_d69995dd} supports Conditioning, Bounded Disjunction, it also supports Singleton Forgetting \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

OR_C.description: Since Negation ∧ Disjunction implies Conjunction \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_d69995dd}{AND_C} \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since Negation is supported in polynomial time, then Disjunction is unsupported by OBDD$_<$ as well.

languages.definition: The set of all OBDD formulas following a fixed variable order $<$, which is known in advance. This strictness allows the equivalence of two distinct functions to be checked in constant time \citet{Bryant_1986}.

languages.name: PI

CE.description: By definition, \langref{PI} is the set of prime implicates of a formula. A clause $\gamma$ is entailed by $\Sigma$ iff $\gamma$ subsumes (is a weakening of) some prime implicate of $\Sigma$. This subsumption check runs in polynomial time (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 4.1, Table 24).

CT.description: The model counting problem for monotone Krom formulas (conjunctions of clauses with at most two positive literals) is \#P-complete \citet{Roth_1996}. Such formulas can be converted to prime implicates form in polynomial time, so \langref{PI} does not satisfy CT (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 4.1, Table 24).

SE.description: $PI \subseteq CNF$ and \langref{PI} satisfies CE, so checking $\Sigma_1 \models \Sigma_2$ for \langref{CNF} $\Sigma_2$ reduces to checking each clause of $\Sigma_2$ against the prime implicates of $\Sigma_1$. Two formulas are equivalent iff they share the same prime implicates (canonical form), giving SE (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 4.1, Table 24).

VA.description: \edgeref{lang_27fffab2}{lang_89649e36} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_89649e36}{VA} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. Therefore PI supports Validity in polynomial time.

IM.description: \edgeref{lang_27fffab2}{lang_89649e36} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_89649e36}{IM} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. Therefore PI supports Implicant in polynomial time.

EQ.description: Since \langref{lang_27fffab2} supports Sentential Entailment, it also supports Equivalence \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

CO.description: Since \langref{lang_27fffab2} supports Clausal Entailment, it also supports Consistency \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

ME.description: Since \langref{lang_27fffab2} supports Consistency, Conditioning, it also supports Model Enumeration \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

NOT_C.description: The formula $\Sigma_n = \bigwedge_{i=0}^{n-1}(x_{2i} \lor x_{2i+1})$ is in PI form but has $2^n$ prime implicants. Its negation has $2^n$ prime implicates. Hence PI cannot satisfy $\neg$C (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

AND_BC.description: Combining prime implicates via conjunction requires distributing and re-minimizing clauses, which can lead to an exponential explosion of new prime implicates. Let $\alpha_1 = \bigvee_{i=1}^k p_i$ (one prime implicate) and $\alpha_2 = \bigwedge_{i=1}^k \bigwedge_{j=1}^m (\neg p_i \lor q_{i,j})$ ($mk$ prime implicates). Then $\alpha_1 \land \alpha_2$ has $(m+1)^k + mk$ prime implicates, an exponential blowup \citet{Chandra_1978} (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

AND_C.description: Since Conjunction implies Bounded Conjunction \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_27fffab2}{AND_BC} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}, then Conjunction is unsupported by PI in quasi-polynomial time as well.

OR_BC.description: $\text{PI}(\alpha_1 \lor \alpha_2) = \min(\{\beta_1 \lor \beta_2 \mid \beta_1 \in \text{PI}(\alpha_1), \beta_2 \in \text{PI}(\alpha_2)\}, \models)$ (Proposition 40, Marquis 2000). The minimization removes subsumed clauses, yielding a PI form in polytime (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

OR_C.description: It is straightforward to convert any term into an equivalent PI formula in polynomial time. Since specific DNF formulas cannot be turned into equivalent PI formulas in polynomial space (DNF $\not\leq$ PI), PI does not satisfy $\lor$C (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

CD.description: The prime implicates of $\Sigma \land \gamma$ can be computed in polytime when $\Sigma$ is in PI form and $\gamma$ is a term (Proposition 36, Marquis 2000). Since PI satisfies FO, the prime implicates of $\Sigma|\gamma \equiv \exists\text{Vars}(\gamma).(\Sigma \land \gamma)$ follow in polytime (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

FO.description: The prime implicates of $\exists X.\Sigma$ are exactly the prime implicates of $\Sigma$ that do not contain any variable from $X$ (Proposition 55, Marquis 2000). Hence FO for PI simply filters out clauses containing forgotten variables (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

SFO.description: Since \langref{lang_27fffab2} supports Forgetting, it also supports Singleton Forgetting \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

languages.definition: A formula represented exactly as the conjunction of all its minimal entailed disjunctive clauses (prime implicates). \citet{Quine_1952}.

languages.name: SDD

CE.description: \edgeref{lang_1afefbe2}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{CE} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2001a}. Therefore SDD supports Clausal Entailment in polynomial time.

CT.description: \edgeref{lang_1afefbe2}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6c130090}{CT} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2001b}. Therefore SDD supports Model Counting in polynomial time.

CO.description: Since \langref{lang_1afefbe2} supports Model Counting, it also supports Consistency \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

VA.description: Since \langref{lang_1afefbe2} supports Model Counting, it also supports Validity \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

IM.description: Since \langref{lang_1afefbe2} supports Negation, Clausal Entailment, it also supports Implicant \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

ME.description: Since \langref{lang_1afefbe2} supports Consistency, Conditioning, it also supports Model Enumeration \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

SE.description: Since \langref{lang_1afefbe2} supports Negation, Bounded Conjunction, Consistency, it also supports Sentential Entailment \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

EQ.description: Since \langref{lang_1afefbe2} supports Sentential Entailment, it also supports Equivalence \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

AND_BC.description: The Apply algorithm combines two SDDs using any Boolean operator in polytime. In particular, two SDDs can be conjoined in polytime via Apply \citet{Darwiche_2011}.

OR_BC.description: The Apply algorithm combines two SDDs using any Boolean operator in polytime. In particular, two SDDs can be disjoined in polytime via Apply \citet{Darwiche_2011}.

NOT_C.description: An SDD can be negated in polytime by applying exclusive-or with $\top$ using the Apply algorithm \citet{Darwiche_2011}.

CD.description: Conditioning an SDD $f$ on a literal $l$ is equivalent to computing $f \land l$. Since any literal is a trivially small SDD and SDD supports $\land$BC in polytime via Apply, conditioning is polytime \citet{Darwiche_2011}.

SFO.description: Since \langref{lang_1afefbe2} supports Conditioning, Bounded Disjunction, it also supports Singleton Forgetting \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

languages.definition: A representation that generalizes OBDDs by replacing binary decision nodes with "primes" and "subs", which are subcircuits also represented as SDDs. At each decision node, there is a set of (mutually exclusive and exhaustive) "primes", each with a corresponding "sub". Evaluating a decision node entails checking which prime is satisfied by the input variables, then evaluating that prime's sub. The SDD respects a vtree $T$ in the same way as an SDNNF \citet{Darwiche_2011}.

languages.fullName: Sentential Decision Diagram (wrt a fixed variable tree)
languages.name: SDD$_T$

CE.description: \edgeref{lang_9c84a267}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{CE} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2001a}. Therefore SDD$_T$ supports Clausal Entailment in polynomial time.

EQ.description: Since \langref{lang_9c84a267} supports Sentential Entailment, it also supports Equivalence \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

CT.description: \edgeref{lang_9c84a267}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6c130090}{CT} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2001b}. Therefore SDD$_T$ supports Model Counting in polynomial time.

CO.description: Since \langref{lang_9c84a267} supports Model Counting, it also supports Consistency \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

VA.description: Since \langref{lang_9c84a267} supports Model Counting, it also supports Validity \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

IM.description: \edgeref{lang_9c84a267}{lang_1afefbe2} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_1afefbe2}{IM} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. Therefore SDD$_T$ supports Implicant in polynomial time.

SE.description: \edgeref{lang_9c84a267}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_ea9b5299}{SE} in polynomial time. Therefore SDD$_T$ supports Sentential Entailment in polynomial time.

ME.description: \edgeref{lang_9c84a267}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6c130090}{ME} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. Therefore SDD$_T$ supports Model Enumeration in polynomial time.

languages.definition: The set of all SDD formulas respecting a fixed variable tree (vtree) $T$. \citet{Darwiche_2011}.

languages.name: SDNNF

CE.description: \edgeref{lang_b13b0d78}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{CE} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2001a}. Therefore SDNNF supports Clausal Entailment in polynomial time.

CO.description: Since \langref{lang_b13b0d78} supports Clausal Entailment, it also supports Consistency \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

ME.description: Since \langref{lang_b13b0d78} supports Consistency, Conditioning, it also supports Model Enumeration \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

SE.description: Since Sentential Entailment implies Equivalence \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_b13b0d78}{EQ} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, then Sentential Entailment is unsupported by SDNNF as well (unless P = NP).

VA.description: \nopref{lang_4c204bf3}{VA} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and \edgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_b13b0d78} in polynomial time. If SDNNF supported Validity in polynomial time, then DNF could too by compiling first. Therefore Validity is unsupported by SDNNF (unless P = NP).

CT.description: \nopref{lang_6ae90adc}{CT} (unless the polynomial hierarchy collapses) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and \edgeref{lang_6ae90adc}{lang_b13b0d78} in polynomial time. If SDNNF supported Model Counting in polynomial time, then IP could too by compiling first. Therefore Model Counting is unsupported by SDNNF (unless the polynomial hierarchy collapses).

EQ.description: Since Equivalence implies Validity \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_b13b0d78}{VA} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, then Equivalence is unsupported by SDNNF as well (unless P = NP).

IM.description: Since Implicant implies Validity \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_b13b0d78}{VA} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, then Implicant is unsupported by SDNNF as well (unless P = NP).

NOT_C.description: Since Negation ∧ Disjunction implies Conjunction \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_b13b0d78}{AND_C} (unless P = NP) \citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}, and since Disjunction is supported in polynomial time, then Negation is unsupported by SDNNF as well (unless P = NP).

AND_BC.description: Bounded conjunction of two \langref{SDNNF} formulas sharing the same vtree can be performed in polytime via the Apply algorithm. The vtree governs variable partitioning at $\land$-nodes, enabling an efficient recursive traversal (\citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}, Proposition 4). Note: requires formulas to follow the same vtree.

FO.description: Forgetting preserves structured decomposability. Replacing all occurrences of literal $x$ and $\neg x$ with $\top$ in an \langref{SDNNF} preserves the vtree structure, since the decomposition at $\land$-nodes remains valid after variable removal. Inherited from \langref{DNNF} (\citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}, Proposition 4).

OR_C.description: Disjunction of \langref{SDNNF} formulas (sharing the same vtree) preserves structured decomposability, since decomposability only constrains $\land$-nodes and a top-level $\lor$-node introduces no new $\land$-node constraints (\citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}, Proposition 4). Note: requires formulas to follow the same vtree.

AND_C.description: Any clause can be trivially expressed as an \langref{SDNNF} formula. If \langref{SDNNF} satisfied $\land$C, any \langref{CNF} could be compiled to \langref{SDNNF} in polytime by conjoining its clauses. Since \langref{SDNNF} satisfies CO, this would give polytime \langref{CNF} satisfiability, implying P = NP (\citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}, Proposition 4).

OR_BC.description: Since \langref{lang_b13b0d78} supports Disjunction, it also supports Bounded Disjunction \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

SFO.description: Since \langref{lang_b13b0d78} supports Forgetting, it also supports Singleton Forgetting \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

CD.description: Since \langref{lang_b13b0d78} supports Bounded Conjunction, Singleton Forgetting, it also supports Conditioning \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

languages.definition: A DNNF that respects a vtree $T$, in the sense that the decomposability of variables across AND nodes is strictly governed by the hierarchical structure of $T$. This guarantees that variable partitions occur systematically \citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}.

languages.fullName: Structured Decomposable Negation Normal Form (wrt a fixed variable tree)
languages.name: SDNNF$_T$

CE.description: \edgeref{lang_3c803ba1}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{CE} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2001a}. Therefore SDNNF$_T$ supports Clausal Entailment in polynomial time.

CO.description: Since \langref{lang_3c803ba1} supports Clausal Entailment, it also supports Consistency \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

ME.description: \edgeref{lang_3c803ba1}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{ME} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. Therefore SDNNF$_T$ supports Model Enumeration in polynomial time.

languages.definition: The set of all SDNNF formulas respecting a fixed vtree $T$. \citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}.

languages.name: uFBDD

CE.description: \edgeref{lang_4e62a038}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{CE} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2001a}. Therefore uFBDD supports Clausal Entailment in polynomial time.

CT.description: \edgeref{lang_4e62a038}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6c130090}{CT} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2001b}. Therefore uFBDD supports Model Counting in polynomial time.

CO.description: Since \langref{lang_4e62a038} supports Model Counting, it also supports Consistency \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

VA.description: Since \langref{lang_4e62a038} supports Model Counting, it also supports Validity \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

IM.description: \edgeref{lang_4e62a038}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6c130090}{IM} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. Therefore uFBDD supports Implicant in polynomial time.

ME.description: \edgeref{lang_4e62a038}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6c130090}{ME} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. Therefore uFBDD supports Model Enumeration in polynomial time.

EQ.description: \edgeref{lang_4e62a038}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6c130090}{EQ} in polynomial time (unless $P \neq$ BPP) \citet{Darwiche_Huang_2002}. Therefore uFBDD supports Equivalence in polynomial time (unless $P \neq$ BPP).

languages.definition: A restricted form of a nondeterministic FBDD where every satisfying assignment has exactly one valid accepting path. This "unambiguity" preserves the ability to perform efficient model counting while allowing for greater compression \citet{Wegener_2000}.

languages.name: uOBDD

CE.description: \edgeref{lang_c2df8c2b}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{CE} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2001a}. Therefore uOBDD supports Clausal Entailment in polynomial time.

CT.description: \edgeref{lang_c2df8c2b}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6c130090}{CT} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2001b}. Therefore uOBDD supports Model Counting in polynomial time.

CO.description: Since \langref{lang_c2df8c2b} supports Model Counting, it also supports Consistency \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

VA.description: Since \langref{lang_c2df8c2b} supports Model Counting, it also supports Validity \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

IM.description: \edgeref{lang_c2df8c2b}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6c130090}{IM} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. Therefore uOBDD supports Implicant in polynomial time.

ME.description: \edgeref{lang_c2df8c2b}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6c130090}{ME} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. Therefore uOBDD supports Model Enumeration in polynomial time.

SE.description: \edgeref{lang_c2df8c2b}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_ea9b5299}{SE} in polynomial time. Therefore uOBDD supports Sentential Entailment in polynomial time.

EQ.description: Since \langref{lang_c2df8c2b} supports Sentential Entailment, it also supports Equivalence \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

languages.definition: A nondeterministic OBDD restricted to unambiguous choices, maintaining efficient counting capabilities while strictly following a global variable order. It provides a middle ground between the strictness of OBDDs and the compression of nondeterminism \citet{Wegener_2000}.

languages.name: OBDD

CT.description: Any query concerning \langref{OBDD} is equivalent to the corresponding query concerning \langfam{OBDD}{<} when only one DAG is involved. Since \langfam{OBDD}{<} satisfies CO, VA and CT, so does \langref{OBDD} (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 4.1, Table 20).

EQ.description: \langref{OBDD} satisfies EQ (Theorem 8.11, Meinel \& Theobald 1998). Any equivalence query on a single \langref{OBDD} reduces to the corresponding query on \langfam{OBDD}{<}, and \langfam{OBDD}{<} supports EQ (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 4.1, Table 20).

SE.description: \edgeref{lang_b9d72a7c}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_ea9b5299}{SE} in polynomial time. Therefore OBDD supports Sentential Entailment in polynomial time.

CO.description: \edgeref{lang_b9d72a7c}{lang_684b1ca7} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_684b1ca7}{CO} in polynomial time \citet{Gergov_1994}. Therefore OBDD supports Consistency in polynomial time.

VA.description: \edgeref{lang_b9d72a7c}{lang_684b1ca7} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_684b1ca7}{VA} in polynomial time \citet{Gergov_1994}. Therefore OBDD supports Validity in polynomial time.

CE.description: \edgeref{lang_b9d72a7c}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{CE} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2001a}. Therefore OBDD supports Clausal Entailment in polynomial time.

ME.description: Since \langref{lang_b9d72a7c} supports Consistency, Conditioning, it also supports Model Enumeration \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

IM.description: Since \langref{lang_b9d72a7c} supports Validity, Conditioning, it also supports Implicant \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

NOT_C.description: Negation of an OBDD is achieved by switching the labels of the 0-sink and 1-sink. The ordered variable property is preserved since the single ordering is not affected (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

AND_BC.description: Checking consistency of two OBDDs (which may possess different underlying orderings) is NP-complete (Lemma 8.14, \citet{Meinel_Theobald_1998}). Since OBDD satisfies CO, if OBDD satisfied $\land$BC consistency could be tested in polytime, implying P = NP (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

CD.description: Since OBDD $\subseteq$ FBDD and FBDD satisfies CD (the restrict operation), OBDD also satisfies CD. The variable ordering is preserved under conditioning (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

FO.description: If OBDD satisfies FO, then every DNF can be compiled to an equivalent OBDD of polynomial size. Since DNF $\not\leq$ OBDD, this is impossible (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

SFO.description: Since only one OBDD sentence is considered in the transformation, and OBDD$_<$ satisfies SFO, OBDD also satisfies SFO (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).

AND_C.description: Since Conjunction implies Bounded Conjunction \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_b9d72a7c}{AND_BC} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, then Conjunction is unsupported by OBDD as well (unless P = NP).

OR_C.description: Since Negation ∧ Disjunction implies Conjunction \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_b9d72a7c}{AND_C} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since Negation is supported in polynomial time, then Disjunction is unsupported by OBDD as well (unless P = NP).

OR_BC.description: Since Negation ∧ Bounded Disjunction implies Bounded Conjunction \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since \nopref{lang_b9d72a7c}{AND_BC} (unless P = NP) \citet{Darwiche_2002}, and since Negation is supported in polynomial time, then Bounded Disjunction is unsupported by OBDD as well (unless P = NP).

languages.definition: A Binary Decision Diagram where all variable decisions must follow a strict, uniform linear order $<$ across every path in the graph. They are strongly canonical and support powerful polytime Boolean operations, making them ubiquitous in formal hardware verification \citet{Bryant_1986}.

languages.name: dec-DNNF

CE.description: \edgeref{lang_981b62f0}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{CE} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2001a}. Therefore dec-DNNF supports Clausal Entailment in polynomial time.

CT.description: \edgeref{lang_981b62f0}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6c130090}{CT} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2001b}. Therefore dec-DNNF supports Model Counting in polynomial time.

CO.description: Since \langref{lang_981b62f0} supports Model Counting, it also supports Consistency \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

VA.description: Since \langref{lang_981b62f0} supports Model Counting, it also supports Validity \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

IM.description: \edgeref{lang_981b62f0}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6c130090}{IM} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. Therefore dec-DNNF supports Implicant in polynomial time.

ME.description: \edgeref{lang_981b62f0}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6c130090}{ME} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. Therefore dec-DNNF supports Model Enumeration in polynomial time.

EQ.description: \edgeref{lang_981b62f0}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6c130090}{EQ} in polynomial time (unless $P \neq$ BPP) \citet{Darwiche_Huang_2002}. Therefore dec-DNNF supports Equivalence in polynomial time (unless $P \neq$ BPP).

languages.definition: A subset of DNNF where standard logical OR nodes are entirely replaced by decision nodes, similar to those found in BDDs. Because it relies explicitly on variable conditioning, it serves as the natural compilation trace of exhaustive DPLL-based SAT solvers \citet{Oztok_2014}.

languages.name: dec-SDNNF
languages.fullName: Structured Decision Decomposable Negation Normal Form

CE.description: \edgeref{lang_0f27d539}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{CE} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2001a}. Therefore dec-SDNNF supports Clausal Entailment in polynomial time.

CT.description: \edgeref{lang_0f27d539}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6c130090}{CT} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2001b}. Therefore dec-SDNNF supports Model Counting in polynomial time.

CO.description: Since \langref{lang_0f27d539} supports Model Counting, it also supports Consistency \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

VA.description: Since \langref{lang_0f27d539} supports Model Counting, it also supports Validity \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

IM.description: \edgeref{lang_0f27d539}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6c130090}{IM} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. Therefore dec-SDNNF supports Implicant in polynomial time.

ME.description: \edgeref{lang_0f27d539}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6c130090}{ME} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. Therefore dec-SDNNF supports Model Enumeration in polynomial time.

EQ.description: Since \langref{lang_0f27d539} supports Sentential Entailment, it also supports Equivalence \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

SE.description: \edgeref{lang_0f27d539}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_ea9b5299}{SE} in polynomial time. Therefore dec-SDNNF supports Sentential Entailment in polynomial time.

languages.definition: A decision DNNF whose underlying variable decisions strictly follow a hierarchical variable tree (vtree). This tree-driven structure standardizes the compilation trace and bridges the gap to sentential decision diagrams \citet{Oztok_2014}.

languages.name: dec-SDNNF$_T$
languages.fullName: Structured Decision Decomposable Negation Normal Form (wrt a fixed variable tree)

CE.description: \edgeref{lang_4ae03bc8}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{CE} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2001a}. Therefore dec-SDNNF$_T$ supports Clausal Entailment in polynomial time.

EQ.description: Since \langref{lang_4ae03bc8} supports Sentential Entailment, it also supports Equivalence \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

CT.description: \edgeref{lang_4ae03bc8}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6c130090}{CT} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2001b}. Therefore dec-SDNNF$_T$ supports Model Counting in polynomial time.

CO.description: Since \langref{lang_4ae03bc8} supports Model Counting, it also supports Consistency \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

VA.description: Since \langref{lang_4ae03bc8} supports Model Counting, it also supports Validity \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

SE.description: \edgeref{lang_4ae03bc8}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_ea9b5299}{SE} in polynomial time. Therefore dec-SDNNF$_T$ supports Sentential Entailment in polynomial time.

IM.description: \edgeref{lang_4ae03bc8}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6c130090}{IM} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. Therefore dec-SDNNF$_T$ supports Implicant in polynomial time.

ME.description: \edgeref{lang_4ae03bc8}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6c130090}{ME} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. Therefore dec-SDNNF$_T$ supports Model Enumeration in polynomial time.

languages.definition: The set of all dec-SDNNF$_T$ formulas that follow a fixed vtree $T$. \citet{Oztok_2014}.

languages.name: cSDD$_T$
languages.fullName: Canonical/Compressed Sentential Decision Diagram (wrt a fixed variable tree)

CE.description: \edgeref{lang_82fa749e}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{CE} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2001a}. Therefore cSDD$_T$ supports Clausal Entailment in polynomial time.

EQ.description: Since \langref{lang_82fa749e} supports Sentential Entailment, it also supports Equivalence \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

CT.description: \edgeref{lang_82fa749e}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6c130090}{CT} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2001b}. Therefore cSDD$_T$ supports Model Counting in polynomial time.

CO.description: Since \langref{lang_82fa749e} supports Model Counting, it also supports Consistency \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

VA.description: Since \langref{lang_82fa749e} supports Model Counting, it also supports Validity \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

IM.description: \edgeref{lang_82fa749e}{lang_1afefbe2} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_1afefbe2}{IM} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. Therefore cSDD$_T$ supports Implicant in polynomial time.

SE.description: \edgeref{lang_82fa749e}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_ea9b5299}{SE} in polynomial time. Therefore cSDD$_T$ supports Sentential Entailment in polynomial time.

ME.description: \edgeref{lang_82fa749e}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6c130090}{ME} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. Therefore cSDD$_T$ supports Model Enumeration in polynomial time.

languages.definition: The set of all cSDD formulas that respect a predefined variable tree (vtree) $T$. \citet{Darwiche_2011}.

languages.name: nOBDD$_<$
languages.fullName: Nondeterministic Ordered Binary Decision Diagram (wrt a fixed variable order)
languages.definition: The set of all nOBDD formulas that follow one fixed variable order $<$. \citet{Wegener_2000}.

CE.description: \edgeref{lang_e827cf31}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{CE} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2001a}. Therefore nOBDD$_<$ supports Clausal Entailment in polynomial time.

CO.description: Since \langref{lang_e827cf31} supports Clausal Entailment, it also supports Consistency \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

ME.description: \edgeref{lang_e827cf31}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{ME} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. Therefore nOBDD$_<$ supports Model Enumeration in polynomial time.

languages.name: uOBDD$_<$
languages.fullName: Unambiguous Ordered Binary Decision Diagram (wrt a fixed variable order)
languages.definition: The set of all uOBDD formulas that follow one fixed variable order $<$. \citet{Wegener_2000}.

CE.description: \edgeref{lang_43a33aec}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{CE} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2001a}. Therefore uOBDD$_<$ supports Clausal Entailment in polynomial time.

EQ.description: Since \langref{lang_43a33aec} supports Sentential Entailment, it also supports Equivalence \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

CT.description: \edgeref{lang_43a33aec}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6c130090}{CT} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2001b}. Therefore uOBDD$_<$ supports Model Counting in polynomial time.

CO.description: Since \langref{lang_43a33aec} supports Model Counting, it also supports Consistency \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

VA.description: Since \langref{lang_43a33aec} supports Model Counting, it also supports Validity \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

SE.description: \edgeref{lang_43a33aec}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_ea9b5299}{SE} in polynomial time. Therefore uOBDD$_<$ supports Sentential Entailment in polynomial time.

IM.description: \edgeref{lang_43a33aec}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6c130090}{IM} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. Therefore uOBDD$_<$ supports Implicant in polynomial time.

ME.description: \edgeref{lang_43a33aec}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6c130090}{ME} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. Therefore uOBDD$_<$ supports Model Enumeration in polynomial time.

@inproceedings{Bodlaender_1993,
title = "A Linear Time Algorithm for Finding Tree-decompositions of Small Treewidth",
abstract = "In this paper, we give, for constant k, a linear time algorithm, that given a graph G = (V, E), determines whether the treewidth of G is at most k, and if so, finds a treedecomposition of G with treewidth at most k. A consequence is that every minor-closed class of graphs that does not contain all planar graphs has a linear time recognition algorithm. ",
keywords = "graph algorithms, graph minors, partial k-trees, pathwidth, treewidth",
author = "Bodlaender, \{Hans L.\}",
year = "1993",
doi = "10.1145/167088.167161",
language = "English",
series = "STOC '93",
publisher = "Association for Computing Machinery",

}

references.bibtex: @article{Bova_2016_a, title={SDDs Are Exponentially More Succinct than OBDDs}, volume={30}, url={https://ojs.aaai.org/index.php/AAAI/article/view/10107}, DOI={10.1609/aaai.v30i1.10107}, abstractNote={ <p> Introduced by Darwiche (2011), sentential decision diagrams (SDDs) are essentially as tractable as ordered binary decision diagrams (OBDDs), but tend to be more succinct in practice. This makes SDDs a prominent representation language, with many applications in artificial intelligence and knowledge compilation. We prove that SDDs are more succinct than OBDDs also in theory, by constructing a family of boolean functions where each member has polynomial SDD size but exponential OBDD size. This exponential separation improves a quasipolynomial separation recently established by Razgon (2014), and settles an open problem in knowledge compilation (Darwiche, 2011). </p> }, number={1}, journal={Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence}, author={Bova, Simone}, year={2016}, month={Feb.} }
references.title: S. Bova, "SDDs Are Exponentially More Succinct than OBDDs", Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence, vol. 30, 2016.
