references.bibtex:
@inproceedings{Darwiche_2011,
author = {Darwiche, Adnan},
title = {SDD: a new canonical representation of propositional knowledge bases},
year = {2011},
isbn = {9781577355144},
publisher = {AAAI Press},
abstract = {We identify a new representation of propositional knowledge bases, the Sentential Decision Diagram (SDD), which is interesting for a number of reasons. First, it is canonical in the presence of additional properties that resemble reduction rules of OBDDs. Second, SDDs can be combined using any Boolean operator in polytime. Third, CNFs with n variables and treewidth w have canonical SDDs of size O(n2w), which is tighter than the bound on OBDDs based on pathwidth. Finally, every OBDD is an SDD. Hence, working with the latter does not preclude the former.},
booktitle = {Proceedings of the Twenty-Second International Joint Conference on Artificial Intelligence - Volume Two},
pages = {819–826},
numpages = {8},
location = {Barcelona, Catalonia, Spain},
series = {IJCAI'11},
doi = {10.5591/978-1-57735-516-8/IJCAI11-143}
}
references.title: A. Darwiche, "SDD: a new canonical representation of propositional knowledge bases", Proceedings of the Twenty-Second International Joint Conference on Artificial Intelligence - Volume Two, pp. 819–826, 2011.
A. Darwiche and J. Huang, "Testing equivalence probabilistically", 2002.

references.bibtex:
@article{Sauerhoff_2003,
title = {Approximation of boolean functions by combinatorial rectangles},
journal = {Theoretical Computer Science},
volume = {301},
number = {1},
pages = {45-78},
year = {2003},
issn = {0304-3975},
doi = {10.1016/S0304-3975(02)00568-6},
url = {https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0304397502005686},
author = {Martin Sauerhoff},
keywords = {Branching programs, Communication complexity, Lower bounds, Approximation, Nondeterminism, Randomness},
abstract = {This paper deals with the number of monochromatic combinatorial rectangles required to approximate a boolean function on a constant fraction of all inputs, where each rectangle may use its own partition of the input variables. The main result of the paper is that the number of rectangles required for the approximation of boolean functions in this model is very sensitive to the allowed error. There is an explicitly defined sequence of boolean functions fn on n variables such that fn has rectangle approximations with a constant number of rectangles and one-sided error 13+o(1) or two-sided error 14+o(1), but, on the other hand, fn requires exponentially many rectangles if the error bounds are decreased by an arbitrarily small constant. As applications of this result, the following separation results for read-once branching programs are obtained. The functions from the main result require only linear size for nondeterministic read-once branching programs and randomized read-once branching programs with two-sided error 13+o(1), while randomized read-once branching programs with constant two-sided error smaller than 13 and unambiguous nondeterministic read-once branching programs require exponential size.}
}
references.title: M. Sauerhoff, "Approximation of boolean functions by combinatorial rectangles", Theoretical Computer Science, vol. 301, pp. 45-78, 2003.

references.bibtex:
@article{Selman_1996,
author = {Selman, Bart and Kautz, Henry},
title = {Knowledge compilation and theory approximation},
year = {1996},
issue_date = {March 1996},
publisher = {Association for Computing Machinery},
address = {New York, NY, USA},
volume = {43},
number = {2},
issn = {0004-5411},
url = {https://doi.org/10.1145/226643.226644},
doi = {10.1145/226643.226644},
abstract = {Computational efficiency is a central concern in the design of knowledge representation systems. In order to obtain efficient systems, it has been suggested that one should limit the form of the statements in the knowledge base or use an incomplete inference mechanism. The former approach is often too restrictive for practical applications, whereas the latter leads to uncertainty about exactly what can and cannot be inferred from the knowledge base. We present a third alternative, in which knowledge given in a general representation language is translated (compiled) into a tractable form—allowing for efficient subsequent query answering.We show how propositional logical theories can be compiled into Horn theories that approximate the original information. The approximations bound the original theory from below and above in terms of logical strength. The procedures are extended to other tractable languages (for example, binary clauses) and to the first-order case. Finally, we demonstrate the generality of our approach by compiling concept descriptions in a general frame-based language into a tractable form.},
journal = {J. ACM},
month = mar,
pages = {193–224},
numpages = {32},
keywords = {Horn clauses, efficient reasoning methods, knowledge compilation, knowledge-base optimization, query evaluation, theory approximation}
}
references.title: B. Selman and H. Kautz, "Knowledge compilation and theory approximation", J. ACM, vol. 43, pp. 193–224, 1996.
G. Van den Broeck and A. Darwiche, "On the Role of Canonicity in Knowledge Compilation", Proceedings of the Twenty-Ninth AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence, pp. 1641–1648, 2015.
H. Vinall-Smeeth, "Structured d-DNNF Is Not Closed Under Negation", Proceedings of the 33rd International Joint Conference on Artificial Intelligence (IJCAI-24), pp. 3593–3601, 2024.

smoothness.label: Smoothness
smoothness.description: All disjuncts mention the same variables

read-once.label: Read-Once
read-once.description: Each variable appears at most once on any path

CO.label: Consistency
CO.description: Check if the formula is satisfiable

VA.label: Validity
VA.description: Check if the formula is a tautology

CE.label: Clausal Entailment
CE.description: Check if a clause is entailed

IM.label: Implicant
IM.description: Find an implicant of the formula

EQ.label: Equivalence
EQ.description: Check logical equivalence of two formulas

SE.label: Sentential Entailment
SE.description: Check if one formula entails another

CT.label: Model Counting
CT.description: Count the number of satisfying assignments

ME.label: Model Enumeration
ME.description: Enumerate all satisfying assignments

matrix.description: \edgeref{lang_27fffab2}{lang_89649e36} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015,Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_27fffab2}{lang_3bebcab7} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Bova_2016}. If CNF compiled to cSDD in quasi-polynomial time, then PI would compile to DNNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore CNF cannot compile to cSDD in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_27fffab2}{lang_89649e36} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \edgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \nedgeref{lang_27fffab2}{lang_3bebcab7} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Bova_2016}. If CNF compiled to d-DNNF in quasi-polynomial time, then PI would compile to DNNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore CNF cannot compile to d-DNNF in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_89649e36}{lang_6c130090} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. If CNF compiled to d-SDNNF in quasi-polynomial time, then CNF would compile to d-DNNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore CNF cannot compile to d-SDNNF in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_91f812d0}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time \citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008,Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_89649e36}{lang_6c130090} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. If CNF compiled to d-SDNNF$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time, then CNF would compile to d-DNNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore CNF cannot compile to d-SDNNF$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_27fffab2}{lang_89649e36} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \nedgeref{lang_27fffab2}{lang_4c204bf3} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. If CNF compiled to DNF in quasi-polynomial time, then PI would compile to DNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore CNF cannot compile to DNF in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_27fffab2}{lang_89649e36} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \nedgeref{lang_27fffab2}{lang_3bebcab7} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Bova_2016}. If CNF compiled to DNNF in quasi-polynomial time, then PI would compile to DNNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore CNF cannot compile to DNNF in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_684b1ca7}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \nedgeref{lang_89649e36}{lang_6c130090} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. If CNF compiled to FBDD in quasi-polynomial time, then CNF would compile to d-DNNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore CNF cannot compile to FBDD in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_6ae90adc}{lang_4c204bf3} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \nedgeref{lang_89649e36}{lang_4c204bf3} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. If CNF compiled to IP in quasi-polynomial time, then CNF would compile to DNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore CNF cannot compile to IP in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_e02902d0}{lang_83e3b023} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Darwiche_2011}. \nedgeref{lang_89649e36}{lang_83e3b023} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,VanDenBroeck_2015,Amarilli_2018}. If CNF compiled to MODS in quasi-polynomial time, then CNF would compile to cSDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore CNF cannot compile to MODS in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_1df07cc3}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_89649e36}{lang_3bebcab7} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. If CNF compiled to nFBDD in quasi-polynomial time, then CNF would compile to DNNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore CNF cannot compile to nFBDD in quasi-polynomial time.
As defined by \citet{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{CNF} is a subset of \langref{NNF}. Therefore, a polynomial compilation is trivial.

\edgeref{lang_d24efe0e}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_89649e36}{lang_3bebcab7} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. If CNF compiled to nOBDD in quasi-polynomial time, then CNF would compile to DNNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore CNF cannot compile to nOBDD in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_d69995dd}{lang_83e3b023} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Darwiche_2011}. \nedgeref{lang_89649e36}{lang_83e3b023} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,VanDenBroeck_2015,Amarilli_2018}. If CNF compiled to OBDD$_<$ in quasi-polynomial time, then CNF would compile to cSDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore CNF cannot compile to OBDD$_<$ in quasi-polynomial time.
It is well-known that some \langref{CNF} formulas (i.e. the negation of the parity function $\neg PARITY_n=\neg\bigoplus_{i=1}^n x_i$) have exponentially many prime implicants.

\edgeref{lang_1afefbe2}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015,Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_89649e36}{lang_6c130090} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. If CNF compiled to SDD in quasi-polynomial time, then CNF would compile to d-DNNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore CNF cannot compile to SDD in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_9c84a267}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2011,VanDenBroeck_2015,Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_89649e36}{lang_6c130090} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. If CNF compiled to SDD$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time, then CNF would compile to d-DNNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. As defined by \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015}, \langref{cSDD} is the compressed subset of \langref{SDD}, so compilation is trivial.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_1afefbe2} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015}. \edgeref{lang_1afefbe2}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015}. \edgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_b13b0d78} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. Therefore cSDD compiles to SDNNF in polynomial time.

Therefore cSDD compiles to uOBDD in quasi-polynomial time.

Theorem 4 of \citet{Bova_2016_a} constructs a generalized hidden weighted bit function $F_n$ on $2n+1$ variables with \langref{cSDD} size $O(n^3)$ but \langref{OBDD} size $2^{\Omega(n)}$. The function $F_n$ augments $HWB_n$ with fresh variables $y_0, \ldots, y_n$ to ensure the resulting SDD is compressed; conditioning $F_n$ on $y_i = 1$ recovers $HWB_n$, preserving Bryant's exponential \langref{OBDD} lower bound.

\edgeref{lang_0f27d539}{lang_b9d72a7c} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015,Darwiche_2002}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_b9d72a7c} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Bova_2016_a}. If cSDD compiled to dec-SDNNF in quasi-polynomial time, then cSDD would compile to OBDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore cSDD cannot compile to dec-SDNNF in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_4ae03bc8}{lang_b9d72a7c} in at worst quasi-polynomial time \citet{Oztok_2014,Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_b9d72a7c} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Bova_2016_a}. If cSDD compiled to dec-SDNNF$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time, then cSDD would compile to OBDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore cSDD cannot compile to dec-SDNNF$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015,Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_89649e36} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Darwiche_2011}. If d-DNNF compiled to CNF in quasi-polynomial time, then cSDD would compile to CNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore d-DNNF cannot compile to CNF in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_684b1ca7}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_1afefbe2} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015}. \nedgeref{lang_684b1ca7}{lang_1afefbe2} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Beame_2015}. If d-DNNF compiled to cSDD in quasi-polynomial time, then FBDD would compile to SDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore d-DNNF cannot compile to cSDD in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_684b1ca7}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \edgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_b13b0d78} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_684b1ca7}{lang_b13b0d78} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Capelli_2016,Pipatsrisawat_2010}. If d-DNNF compiled to d-SDNNF in quasi-polynomial time, then FBDD would compile to SDNNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore d-DNNF cannot compile to d-SDNNF in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_91f812d0}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}. \nedgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_ea9b5299} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Amarilli_2018}. If d-DNNF compiled to d-SDNNF$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time, then d-DNNF would compile to d-SDNNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore d-DNNF cannot compile to d-SDNNF$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015,Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_4c204bf3} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Darwiche_2011}. If d-DNNF compiled to DNF in quasi-polynomial time, then cSDD would compile to DNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore d-DNNF cannot compile to DNF in quasi-polynomial time.

As formalized by \citet{Darwiche_2002} and originally introduced in \citet{Darwiche_2001a}, \langref{d-DNNF} is a subset of \langref{DNNF} satisfying the determinism property, making a polynomial compilation trivial.

\langref{d-DNNF}s can polynomially represent the disjunction of two functions with conflicting variable orders, which forces \langref{FBDD}s to grow exponentially to track the state of both orders within a single "read-once" path.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015,Amarilli_2018}. \edgeref{lang_6ae90adc}{lang_4c204bf3} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_4c204bf3} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Darwiche_2011}. If d-DNNF compiled to IP in quasi-polynomial time, then cSDD would compile to DNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore d-DNNF cannot compile to IP in quasi-polynomial time.
The $OR_n$ function $\bigvee_{i=1}^nx_i$ can be represented in $O(n)$ as a \langref{d-DNNF} formula but has $2^n-1$ models.

Now, we show a quasi-polynomial compilation exists.

quasiDescription.description: The quasi-polynomial compilation from $dec$-$DNNF$ to $FBDD$ yields a $uFBDD$ when applied to a $d$-$DNNF$.

matrix.description:
First, we show no polynomial compilation exists.
\edgeref{lang_4e62a038}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Beame_2013}. If d-DNNF compiled to uFBDD in polynomial time, then d-DNNF would compile to nFBDD in polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore d-DNNF cannot compile to uFBDD in polynomial time.

Now, we show a quasi-polynomial compilation exists.
The quasi-polynomial compilation from $dec$-$DNNF$ to $FBDD$ yields a $uFBDD$ when applied to a $d$-$DNNF$.

\edgeref{lang_c2df8c2b}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_ea9b5299} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Amarilli_2018}. If d-DNNF compiled to uOBDD in quasi-polynomial time, then d-DNNF would compile to d-SDNNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore d-DNNF cannot compile to uOBDD in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015,Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_b9d72a7c} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Bova_2016_a}. If d-DNNF compiled to OBDD in quasi-polynomial time, then cSDD would compile to OBDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore d-DNNF cannot compile to OBDD in quasi-polynomial time.
Shown by Corollary 3.5 of \citet{Beame_2013}.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015,Amarilli_2018}. \edgeref{lang_0f27d539}{lang_b9d72a7c} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015,Darwiche_2002}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_b9d72a7c} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Bova_2016_a}. If d-DNNF compiled to dec-SDNNF in quasi-polynomial time, then cSDD would compile to OBDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore d-DNNF cannot compile to dec-SDNNF in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015,Amarilli_2018}. \edgeref{lang_4ae03bc8}{lang_b9d72a7c} in at worst quasi-polynomial time \citet{Oztok_2014,Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_b9d72a7c} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Bova_2016_a}. \edgeref{lang_e827cf31}{lang_d24efe0e} in polynomial time \citet{Wegener_2000}. \nedgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_d24efe0e} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Amarilli_2018}. Therefore d-DNNF cannot compile to nOBDD$_<$ in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_43a33aec}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{Wegener_2000,Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_ea9b5299} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Amarilli_2018}. Therefore d-DNNF cannot compile to uOBDD$_<$ in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_89649e36} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Darwiche_2011}. \edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_1afefbe2} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015}. \nedgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_1afefbe2} in polynomial time \citet{Vinall-Smeeth_2024}. If d-SDNNF compiled to cSDD in polynomial time, then d-SDNNF would compile to SDD in polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore d-SDNNF cannot compile to cSDD in polynomial time.
As defined by \citet{Amarilli_2018}, a \langref{d-SDNNF} is simply a structured \langref{d-DNNF}, so compilation is trivial.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_4c204bf3} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Darwiche_2011}. Therefore d-SDNNF cannot compile to DNF in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_b13b0d78} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \edgeref{lang_b13b0d78}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. Therefore d-SDNNF compiles to DNNF in polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_1afefbe2}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015}. \nedgeref{lang_1afefbe2}{lang_684b1ca7} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Bollig_2018}. Therefore d-SDNNF cannot compile to FBDD in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015}. \edgeref{lang_6ae90adc}{lang_4c204bf3} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_4c204bf3} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Darwiche_2011}. Therefore d-SDNNF cannot compile to IP in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015}. \edgeref{lang_e02902d0}{lang_89649e36} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_89649e36} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Darwiche_2011}. If d-SDNNF compiled to MODS in quasi-polynomial time, then cSDD would compile to CNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore d-SDNNF cannot compile to MODS in quasi-polynomial time.

matrix.description:
First, we show no polynomial compilation exists.
\edgeref{lang_0f27d539}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_0f27d539}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Beame_2013}. If d-SDNNF compiled to nFBDD in polynomial time, then dec-SDNNF would compile to nFBDD in polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore d-SDNNF cannot compile to nFBDD in polynomial time.

Now, we show a quasi-polynomial compilation exists.
\edgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_c2df8c2b} in at worst quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015}. \edgeref{lang_c2df8c2b}{lang_4e62a038} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \edgeref{lang_4e62a038}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. Therefore d-SDNNF compiles to nFBDD in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_b13b0d78} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \edgeref{lang_b13b0d78}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \edgeref{lang_3bebcab7}{lang_5bf00851} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. Therefore d-SDNNF compiles to NNF in polynomial time.

If d-SDNNF compiled to nOBDD in polynomial time, then d-SDNNF would compile to nFBDD in polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore d-SDNNF cannot compile to nOBDD in polynomial time.

matrix.description:
First, we show no polynomial compilation exists.
\edgeref{lang_d24efe0e}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015}. If d-SDNNF compiled to nOBDD in polynomial time, then d-SDNNF would compile to nFBDD in polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore d-SDNNF cannot compile to nOBDD in polynomial time.

Now, we show a quasi-polynomial compilation exists.
\edgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_c2df8c2b} in at worst quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015}. \edgeref{lang_c2df8c2b}{lang_d24efe0e} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. Therefore d-SDNNF compiles to nOBDD in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_d69995dd} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. If d-SDNNF compiled to OBDD$_<$ in quasi-polynomial time, then cSDD would compile to OBDD$_<$ in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore d-SDNNF cannot compile to OBDD$_<$ in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015}. \edgeref{lang_27fffab2}{lang_89649e36} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_89649e36} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Darwiche_2011}. If d-SDNNF compiled to PI in quasi-polynomial time, then cSDD would compile to CNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore d-SDNNF cannot compile to PI in quasi-polynomial time.
Theorem 1 of \citet{Vinall-Smeeth_2024} proved that there exists a family of functions $f_n$ with \langref{d-SDNNF} of size $n$ but any \langref{SDD} representing $f_n$ has size $n^{\Omega(log(n))}$.

\edgeref{lang_9c84a267}{lang_1afefbe2} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2011}. \nedgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_1afefbe2} in polynomial time \citet{Vinall-Smeeth_2024}. If d-SDNNF compiled to SDD$_T$ in polynomial time, then d-SDNNF would compile to SDD in polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore d-SDNNF cannot compile to SDD$_T$ in polynomial time.
As defined by \citet{Amarilli_2018}, \langref{d-SDNNF} is simply the subset of \langref{SDNNF} satisfying determinism.

noPolyDescription.description: \edgeref{lang_4e62a038}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015}. If d-SDNNF compiled to uFBDD in polynomial time, then d-SDNNF would compile to nFBDD in polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore d-SDNNF cannot compile to uFBDD in polynomial time.

matrix.description:
First, we show no polynomial compilation exists.
\edgeref{lang_4e62a038}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015}. If d-SDNNF compiled to uFBDD in polynomial time, then d-SDNNF would compile to nFBDD in polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore d-SDNNF cannot compile to uFBDD in polynomial time.

Now, we show a quasi-polynomial compilation exists.
\edgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_c2df8c2b} in at worst quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015}. \edgeref{lang_c2df8c2b}{lang_4e62a038} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. Therefore d-SDNNF compiles to uFBDD in quasi-polynomial time.

noPolyDescription.description: \edgeref{lang_c2df8c2b}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015}. If d-SDNNF compiled to uOBDD in polynomial time, then d-SDNNF would compile to nFBDD in polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore d-SDNNF cannot compile to uOBDD in polynomial time.

quasiDescription.description: Theorem 2 of \citet{Bollig_2018} shows that \langref{SDNNF} can be compiled into \langref{nOBDD} by adapting the compilation of \citet{Beame_2015} from \langref{DNNF} to \langref{nFBDD}; proposition 2 of \citet{Bollig_2018} shows that this \langref{nOBDD} is unambiguous if the \langref{SDNNF} is deterministic.

matrix.description:
First, we show no polynomial compilation exists.
\edgeref{lang_c2df8c2b}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015}. If d-SDNNF compiled to uOBDD in polynomial time, then d-SDNNF would compile to nFBDD in polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore d-SDNNF cannot compile to uOBDD in polynomial time.

Now, we show a quasi-polynomial compilation exists.
Theorem 2 of \citet{Bollig_2018} shows that \langref{SDNNF} can be compiled into \langref{nOBDD} by adapting the compilation of \citet{Beame_2015} from \langref{DNNF} to \langref{nFBDD}; proposition 2 of \citet{Bollig_2018} shows that this \langref{nOBDD} is unambiguous if the \langref{SDNNF} is deterministic.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_b9d72a7c} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Bova_2016_a}. If d-SDNNF compiled to OBDD in quasi-polynomial time, then cSDD would compile to OBDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore d-SDNNF cannot compile to OBDD in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_981b62f0}{lang_684b1ca7} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Beame_2013}. \nedgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_684b1ca7} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015}. If d-SDNNF compiled to dec-DNNF in quasi-polynomial time, then d-SDNNF would compile to FBDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore d-SDNNF cannot compile to dec-DNNF in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015}. \edgeref{lang_0f27d539}{lang_b9d72a7c} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015,Darwiche_2002}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_b9d72a7c} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Bova_2016_a}. If d-SDNNF compiled to dec-SDNNF in quasi-polynomial time, then cSDD would compile to OBDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore d-SDNNF cannot compile to dec-SDNNF in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015}. \edgeref{lang_4ae03bc8}{lang_b9d72a7c} in at worst quasi-polynomial time \citet{Oztok_2014,Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_b9d72a7c} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Bova_2016_a}. If d-SDNNF compiled to dec-SDNNF$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time, then cSDD would compile to OBDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore d-SDNNF cannot compile to dec-SDNNF$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_82fa749e}{lang_83e3b023} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2011}. \nedgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_83e3b023} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015}. If d-SDNNF compiled to cSDD$_T$ in polynomial time, then d-SDNNF would compile to cSDD in polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore d-SDNNF cannot compile to cSDD$_T$ in polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_e827cf31}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time \citet{Wegener_2000,Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015}. If d-SDNNF compiled to nOBDD$_<$ in polynomial time, then d-SDNNF would compile to nFBDD in polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore d-SDNNF cannot compile to nOBDD$_<$ in polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_43a33aec}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time \citet{Wegener_2000,Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015}. If d-SDNNF compiled to uOBDD$_<$ in polynomial time, then d-SDNNF would compile to nFBDD in polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore d-SDNNF cannot compile to uOBDD$_<$ in polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_91f812d0}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}. \edgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. Therefore d-SDNNF$_T$ compiles to d-DNNF in polynomial time.

\langref{d-SDNNF} is the union of the fixed-vtree languages \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}. Hence any \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} formula is already a \langref{d-SDNNF} formula, so the compilation is trivial and polynomial-time.

\edgeref{lang_91f812d0}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}. \edgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_b13b0d78} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \edgeref{lang_b13b0d78}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. Therefore d-SDNNF$_T$ compiles to DNNF in polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_91f812d0}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}. \edgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_c2df8c2b} in at worst quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015}. \edgeref{lang_c2df8c2b}{lang_4e62a038} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \edgeref{lang_4e62a038}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. Therefore d-SDNNF$_T$ compiles to nFBDD in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_91f812d0}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}. \edgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_b13b0d78} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \edgeref{lang_b13b0d78}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \edgeref{lang_3bebcab7}{lang_5bf00851} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. Therefore d-SDNNF$_T$ compiles to NNF in polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_91f812d0}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}. \edgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_c2df8c2b} in at worst quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015}. \edgeref{lang_c2df8c2b}{lang_d24efe0e} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. Therefore d-SDNNF$_T$ compiles to nOBDD in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_91f812d0}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}. \edgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_b13b0d78} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. Therefore d-SDNNF$_T$ compiles to SDNNF in polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_91f812d0}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}. \edgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_c2df8c2b} in at worst quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015}. \edgeref{lang_c2df8c2b}{lang_4e62a038} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. Therefore d-SDNNF$_T$ compiles to uFBDD in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_91f812d0}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}. \edgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_c2df8c2b} in at worst quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015}. Therefore d-SDNNF$_T$ compiles to uOBDD in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_6ae90adc}{lang_4c204bf3} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \nedgeref{lang_6ae90adc}{lang_89649e36} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. If DNF compiled to CNF in quasi-polynomial time, then IP would compile to CNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore DNF cannot compile to CNF in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015}. \nedgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_ea9b5299} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. If DNF compiled to cSDD in quasi-polynomial time, then DNF would compile to d-SDNNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore DNF cannot compile to cSDD in quasi-polynomial time.

Because counting the models of a \langref{d-DNNF} is in P and counting the models of a \langref{DNF} is \#P-complete, \langref{DNF} cannot be polynomially compiled into \langref{d-DNNF} unconditionally. Such a compilation implies P = \#P, collapsing the polynomial hierarchy \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

Corollary 7.6 of \citet{Amarilli_2018} gives a lower bound of $2^{\Omega(tw(\phi))}$ on the smallest \langref{d-SDNNF} computing any monotone \langref{DNF} of constant arity and degree. In particular, DNF families whose treewidth grows linearly with their size force exponential-size \langref{d-SDNNF} representations.

\edgeref{lang_91f812d0}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}. \nedgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_ea9b5299} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. If DNF compiled to d-SDNNF$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time, then DNF would compile to d-SDNNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore DNF cannot compile to d-SDNNF$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time.
As defined by \citet{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{DNF} is a subset of \langref{DNNF}. Therefore, a polynomial compilation is trivial.

\edgeref{lang_6ae90adc}{lang_4c204bf3} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \nedgeref{lang_6ae90adc}{lang_684b1ca7} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Wegener_1987}. If DNF compiled to FBDD in quasi-polynomial time, then IP would compile to FBDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore DNF cannot compile to FBDD in quasi-polynomial time.
It is well-known that some \langref{DNF} formulas (i.e. the parity function $PARITY_n=\bigoplus_{i=1}^n x_i$) have exponentially many prime implicates.

\edgeref{lang_e02902d0}{lang_89649e36} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \nedgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_89649e36} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. If DNF compiled to MODS in quasi-polynomial time, then DNF would compile to CNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore DNF cannot compile to MODS in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_d24efe0e} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Wegener_2000}. \edgeref{lang_d24efe0e}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. Therefore DNF compiles to nFBDD in polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \edgeref{lang_3bebcab7}{lang_5bf00851} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. Therefore DNF compiles to NNF in polynomial time.

Fix any variable order $<$. Each term of a \langref{DNF} can be represented as an \langfam{OBDD}{<} of linear size (a chain of decision nodes for each literal in the term). The nondeterministic disjunction of these \langfam{OBDD}{<}s yields an \langref{nOBDD} of size $O(|\phi|)$, since the variable order is shared across all branches \citet{Amarilli_2018,Wegener_2000}.

\edgeref{lang_d69995dd}{lang_83e3b023} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Darwiche_2011}. \nedgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_83e3b023} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015}. If DNF compiled to OBDD$_<$ in quasi-polynomial time, then DNF would compile to cSDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore DNF cannot compile to OBDD$_<$ in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_27fffab2}{lang_89649e36} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \nedgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_89649e36} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. If DNF compiled to PI in quasi-polynomial time, then DNF would compile to CNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore DNF cannot compile to PI in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_1afefbe2}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015}. \nedgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_ea9b5299} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. If DNF compiled to SDD in quasi-polynomial time, then DNF would compile to d-SDNNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore DNF cannot compile to SDD in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_9c84a267}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2011,VanDenBroeck_2015}. \nedgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_ea9b5299} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. If DNF compiled to SDD$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time, then DNF would compile to d-SDNNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore DNF cannot compile to SDD$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_d24efe0e} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Wegener_2000}. \edgeref{lang_d24efe0e}{lang_b13b0d78} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. Therefore DNF compiles to SDNNF in polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_4e62a038}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time (unless the polynomial hierarchy collapses) \citet{Darwiche_2002}. If DNF compiled to uFBDD in polynomial time, then DNF would compile to d-DNNF in polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore DNF cannot compile to uFBDD in polynomial time (unless the polynomial hierarchy collapses).

\edgeref{lang_c2df8c2b}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_ea9b5299} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. If DNF compiled to uOBDD in quasi-polynomial time, then DNF would compile to d-SDNNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore DNF cannot compile to uOBDD in quasi-polynomial time.

\citet{Devadas_1993} showed that there exist Boolean functions with polynomial-size \langref{DNF} representations but requiring exponential-size \langref{OBDD} representations for any variable ordering. This establishes $P(\langref{DNF}) \not\subseteq P(\langref{OBDD})$.

\edgeref{lang_981b62f0}{lang_684b1ca7} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Beame_2013}. \nedgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_684b1ca7} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. If DNF compiled to dec-DNNF in quasi-polynomial time, then DNF would compile to FBDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore DNF cannot compile to dec-DNNF in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_0f27d539}{lang_83e3b023} in at worst quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015,Darwiche_2011}. \nedgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_83e3b023} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015}. If DNF compiled to dec-SDNNF in quasi-polynomial time, then DNF would compile to cSDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore DNF cannot compile to dec-SDNNF in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_4ae03bc8}{lang_83e3b023} in at worst quasi-polynomial time \citet{Oztok_2014,Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015,Darwiche_2011}. \nedgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_83e3b023} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015}. If DNF compiled to dec-SDNNF$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time, then DNF would compile to cSDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore DNF cannot compile to dec-SDNNF$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_82fa749e}{lang_83e3b023} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2011}. \nedgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_83e3b023} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015}. If DNF compiled to cSDD$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time, then DNF would compile to cSDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore DNF cannot compile to cSDD$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_43a33aec}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{Wegener_2000,Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_ea9b5299} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. If DNF compiled to uOBDD$_<$ in quasi-polynomial time, then DNF would compile to d-SDNNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore DNF cannot compile to uOBDD$_<$ in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015,Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_89649e36} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Darwiche_2011}. If DNNF compiled to CNF in quasi-polynomial time, then cSDD would compile to CNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore DNNF cannot compile to CNF in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \nedgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_83e3b023} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Amarilli_2018,VanDenBroeck_2015}. If DNNF compiled to cSDD in quasi-polynomial time, then d-DNNF would compile to cSDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore DNNF cannot compile to cSDD in quasi-polynomial time.

Proposition 7 of \citet{Bova_2016} shows that the Sauerhoff function $S_n$ has \langref{DNNF} size $O(n^2)$. Theorem 9 shows that $S_n$ has \langref{d-DNNF} size $2^{\Omega(n)}$. (Also see \citet{Bova_2014}.)

\edgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \nedgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_ea9b5299} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Amarilli_2018}. If DNNF compiled to d-SDNNF in quasi-polynomial time, then d-DNNF would compile to d-SDNNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore DNNF cannot compile to d-SDNNF in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \edgeref{lang_91f812d0}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}. \nedgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_ea9b5299} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Amarilli_2018}. If DNNF compiled to d-SDNNF$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time, then d-DNNF would compile to d-SDNNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore DNNF cannot compile to d-SDNNF$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015,Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_4c204bf3} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Darwiche_2011}. If DNNF compiled to DNF in quasi-polynomial time, then cSDD would compile to DNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore DNNF cannot compile to DNF in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \nedgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_684b1ca7} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2000}. If DNNF compiled to FBDD in quasi-polynomial time, then d-DNNF would compile to FBDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore DNNF cannot compile to FBDD in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015,Amarilli_2018}. \edgeref{lang_6ae90adc}{lang_4c204bf3} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_4c204bf3} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Darwiche_2011}. If DNNF compiled to IP in quasi-polynomial time, then cSDD would compile to DNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore DNNF cannot compile to IP in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015,Amarilli_2018}. \edgeref{lang_e02902d0}{lang_89649e36} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_89649e36} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Darwiche_2011}. If DNNF compiled to MODS in quasi-polynomial time, then cSDD would compile to CNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore DNNF cannot compile to MODS in quasi-polynomial time.

noPolyDescription.description: \edgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \nedgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Beame_2013}. If DNNF compiled to nFBDD in polynomial time, then d-DNNF would compile to nFBDD in polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore DNNF cannot compile to nFBDD in polynomial time.

quasiDescription.description: Section 5 of \citet{Bodlaender_1993} extends the compilation of \langref{dec-DNNF}s into \langref{FBDD}s (shown in \citet{Beame_2013}) to show that \langref{DNNF} can be quasi-polynomial-time compiled into \langref{nFBDD}.

matrix.description:
First, we show no polynomial compilation exists.
\edgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \nedgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Beame_2013}. If DNNF compiled to nFBDD in polynomial time, then d-DNNF would compile to nFBDD in polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore DNNF cannot compile to nFBDD in polynomial time.

Now, we show a quasi-polynomial compilation exists.
Section 5 of \citet{Bodlaender_1993} extends the compilation of \langref{dec-DNNF}s into \langref{FBDD}s (shown in \citet{Beame_2013}) to show that \langref{DNNF} can be quasi-polynomial-time compiled into \langref{nFBDD}.
As defined by \citet{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{DNNF} is a subset of \langref{NNF}, so a polynomial compilation is trivial.

\edgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \nedgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_d24efe0e} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Amarilli_2018}. If DNNF compiled to nOBDD in quasi-polynomial time, then d-DNNF would compile to nOBDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore DNNF cannot compile to nOBDD in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015,Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_d69995dd} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. If DNNF compiled to OBDD$_<$ in quasi-polynomial time, then cSDD would compile to OBDD$_<$ in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore DNNF cannot compile to OBDD$_<$ in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015,Amarilli_2018}. \edgeref{lang_27fffab2}{lang_89649e36} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_89649e36} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Darwiche_2011}. If DNNF compiled to PI in quasi-polynomial time, then cSDD would compile to CNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore DNNF cannot compile to PI in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \edgeref{lang_1afefbe2}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015}. \nedgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_ea9b5299} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Amarilli_2018}. If DNNF compiled to SDD in quasi-polynomial time, then d-DNNF would compile to d-SDNNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore DNNF cannot compile to SDD in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \edgeref{lang_9c84a267}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2011,VanDenBroeck_2015}. \nedgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_ea9b5299} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Amarilli_2018}. If DNNF compiled to SDD$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time, then d-DNNF would compile to d-SDNNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore DNNF cannot compile to SDD$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \edgeref{lang_b13b0d78}{lang_d24efe0e} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015}. \nedgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_d24efe0e} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Amarilli_2018}. If DNNF compiled to SDNNF in quasi-polynomial time, then d-DNNF would compile to nOBDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore DNNF cannot compile to SDNNF in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \edgeref{lang_3c803ba1}{lang_d24efe0e} in at worst quasi-polynomial time \citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015}. \nedgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_d24efe0e} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Amarilli_2018}. If DNNF compiled to SDNNF$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time, then d-DNNF would compile to nOBDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore DNNF cannot compile to SDNNF$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_4e62a038}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_3bebcab7}{lang_6c130090} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Bova_2016}. If DNNF compiled to uFBDD in quasi-polynomial time, then DNNF would compile to d-DNNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore DNNF cannot compile to uFBDD in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \edgeref{lang_c2df8c2b}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_ea9b5299} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Amarilli_2018}. If DNNF compiled to uOBDD in quasi-polynomial time, then d-DNNF would compile to d-SDNNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore DNNF cannot compile to uOBDD in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015,Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_b9d72a7c} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Bova_2016_a}. If DNNF compiled to OBDD in quasi-polynomial time, then cSDD would compile to OBDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore DNNF cannot compile to OBDD in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \edgeref{lang_981b62f0}{lang_684b1ca7} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Beame_2013}. \nedgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_684b1ca7} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2000}. If DNNF compiled to dec-DNNF in quasi-polynomial time, then d-DNNF would compile to FBDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore DNNF cannot compile to dec-DNNF in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015,Amarilli_2018}. \edgeref{lang_0f27d539}{lang_b9d72a7c} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015,Darwiche_2002}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_b9d72a7c} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Bova_2016_a}. If DNNF compiled to dec-SDNNF in quasi-polynomial time, then cSDD would compile to OBDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore DNNF cannot compile to dec-SDNNF in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015,Amarilli_2018}. \edgeref{lang_4ae03bc8}{lang_b9d72a7c} in at worst quasi-polynomial time \citet{Oztok_2014,Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_b9d72a7c} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Bova_2016_a}. If DNNF compiled to dec-SDNNF$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time, then cSDD would compile to OBDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore DNNF cannot compile to dec-SDNNF$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \edgeref{lang_82fa749e}{lang_83e3b023} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2011}. \nedgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_83e3b023} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Amarilli_2018,VanDenBroeck_2015}. If DNNF compiled to cSDD$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time, then d-DNNF would compile to cSDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore DNNF cannot compile to cSDD$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \edgeref{lang_e827cf31}{lang_d24efe0e} in polynomial time \citet{Wegener_2000}. \nedgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_d24efe0e} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Amarilli_2018}. If DNNF compiled to nOBDD$_<$ in quasi-polynomial time, then d-DNNF would compile to nOBDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore DNNF cannot compile to nOBDD$_<$ in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \edgeref{lang_43a33aec}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{Wegener_2000,Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_ea9b5299} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Amarilli_2018}. If DNNF compiled to uOBDD$_<$ in quasi-polynomial time, then d-DNNF would compile to d-SDNNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore DNNF cannot compile to uOBDD$_<$ in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_d69995dd}{lang_684b1ca7} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \nedgeref{lang_d69995dd}{lang_89649e36} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. If FBDD compiled to CNF in quasi-polynomial time, then OBDD$_<$ would compile to CNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore FBDD cannot compile to CNF in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_1afefbe2} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015}. \nedgeref{lang_684b1ca7}{lang_1afefbe2} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Beame_2015}. If FBDD compiled to cSDD in quasi-polynomial time, then FBDD would compile to SDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore FBDD cannot compile to cSDD in quasi-polynomial time.
As defined by \citet{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{FBDD} is a subset of \langref{d-DNNF}, so a polynomial compilation is trivial.

\edgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_b13b0d78} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_684b1ca7}{lang_b13b0d78} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Capelli_2016,Pipatsrisawat_2010}. If FBDD compiled to d-SDNNF in quasi-polynomial time, then FBDD would compile to SDNNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore FBDD cannot compile to d-SDNNF in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_91f812d0}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}. \nedgeref{lang_684b1ca7}{lang_ea9b5299} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. If FBDD compiled to d-SDNNF$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time, then FBDD would compile to d-SDNNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore FBDD cannot compile to d-SDNNF$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_b13b0d78} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Wegener_2000}. \nedgeref{lang_684b1ca7}{lang_b13b0d78} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Capelli_2016,Pipatsrisawat_2010}. If FBDD compiled to DNF in quasi-polynomial time, then FBDD would compile to SDNNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore FBDD cannot compile to DNF in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_684b1ca7}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \edgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. Therefore FBDD compiles to DNNF in polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_6ae90adc}{lang_4c204bf3} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \nedgeref{lang_684b1ca7}{lang_4c204bf3} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Wegener_2000}. If FBDD compiled to IP in quasi-polynomial time, then FBDD would compile to DNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore FBDD cannot compile to IP in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_e02902d0}{lang_89649e36} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \nedgeref{lang_684b1ca7}{lang_89649e36} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. If FBDD compiled to MODS in quasi-polynomial time, then FBDD would compile to CNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore FBDD cannot compile to MODS in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_684b1ca7}{lang_4e62a038} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \edgeref{lang_4e62a038}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. Therefore FBDD compiles to nFBDD in polynomial time.

Consider the \langref{DNF} formula $\Pi_n= igvee_{i=0}^{n-1}(x_{2i} \wedge x_{2i+1})$. This formula is in prime implicants form and each clause in $\Pi_n$ is an essential prime implicant of it. Hence its negation $
eg\Pi_n\in IP$.

Since the early work of Quine, we know that the number of essential prime implicates of a formula is a lower bound on the number of clauses that can be found in any \langref{CNF} representation of it. However, since $\Pi_n$ has $2^n$ prime implicants, $
eg\Pi_n$ has $2^n$ prime implicates, so any \langref{CNF} representation of $
eg\Pi_n$ must have at least $2^n$ clauses.

Rewrite each decision node labeled $x$ as $(x \wedge D_0) \vee (\neg x \wedge D_1)$, where $D_0, D_1$ are the rewritings of the 0- and 1-successors. The $\wedge$-gates are decomposable, yielding a \langref{DNNF} in linear time \citet{Amarilli_2018}.

Consider the Boolean functions $\psi_n,\phi_n:\{0,1\}^{n^2}\to\{0,1\}$ that respectively test whether, in an $n \times n$ Boolean matrix, either the number of 1's is odd and there is a full row of 1's, or the number of 1's is even and there is a column full of 1's. An \langref{FBDD} of size $O(n^2)$ may simulate either $\psi_n$ or $\phi_n$, and thus the \langref{uFBDD} $\psi_n\vee\phi_n$ is of size $O(n^2)$; however, an \langref{FBDD} for $\psi_n\vee\phi_n$ is provably of size $2^{\Omega(n^{1/2})}$ \citet{Amarilli_2018}.

Construct a right-linear v-tree from the variable order, then rewrite each decision node labeled $x$ as $(x \wedge D_0) \vee (\neg x \wedge D_1)$, where $D_0, D_1$ are the rewritings of the 0- and 1-successors. The v-tree alignment ensures every $\wedge$-gate respects the variable partition, yielding an \langref{SDNNF} in linear time \citet{Amarilli_2018}.

The pairwise CNF $\Sigma_n = \bigwedge_{i=0}^{n-1}(x_{2i} \vee x_{2i+1})$ has $2^n$ prime implicates; its negation $\neg\Sigma_n \in PI$ therefore has $2^n$ essential prime implicants, forcing any \langref{DNF} representation to have at least $2^n$ terms \citet{Darwiche_2002}.

Proposition 11 of \citet{Bova_2016} shows that the $JS_n$ has \langref{PI} size $O(n^2)$. Proposition 12 of \citet{Bova_2016} shows that $JS_n$ has \langref{DNNF} size $2^{\Omega(n^2)}$. (Also see \citet{Bova_2014}.)

The clique detection function $\Sigma_{n,k}$ has $O(n^3)$ prime implicates (hence polynomial \langref{PI} size), but any \langref{FBDD} for $\Sigma_{n,k}$ has exponential size for suitable $k$ \citet{Wegener_1987,Darwiche_2002}.

The $OR_n$ function $\bigvee_{i=1}^nx_i$ can be represented in $O(n)$ as a \langref{PI} formula but has $2^n-1$ models.

The weighted sum function $WS_n$ can be represented by SDDs of size $O(n^3)$ (Corollary 3 of \citet{Bollig_2018}), but has exponential \langref{FBDD} size. Therefore, $P(SDD) \not\subseteq P(FBDD)$, which combined with the known result $P(FBDD) \not\subseteq P(SDD)$ from \citet{Beame_2015}, establishes that \langref{SDD} and \langref{FBDD} are incomparable (Corollary 4 of \citet{Bollig_2018}).

Therefore SDD$_T$ compiles to NNF in polynomial time.

Therefore SDD$_T$ compiles to nOBDD in quasi-polynomial time.
\langref{SDD} is the union of the fixed-vtree languages \langfam{SDD}{T}. Hence any \langfam{SDD}{T} formula is already an \langref{SDD} formula, so the compilation is trivial and polynomial-time.

\edgeref{lang_9c84a267}{lang_1afefbe2} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2011}. \edgeref{lang_1afefbe2}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015}. \edgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_b13b0d78} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. Therefore SDD$_T$ compiles to SDNNF in polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_9c84a267}{lang_1afefbe2} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2011}. \edgeref{lang_1afefbe2}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015}. \edgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_c2df8c2b} in at worst quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015}. \edgeref{lang_c2df8c2b}{lang_4e62a038} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. Therefore SDD$_T$ compiles to uFBDD in quasi-polynomial time.

Therefore SDD$_T$ compiles to uOBDD in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_b13b0d78} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015,Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_89649e36} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Darwiche_2011}. If SDNNF compiled to CNF in quasi-polynomial time, then cSDD would compile to CNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore SDNNF cannot compile to CNF in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_b13b0d78} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Wegener_2000}. \nedgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_83e3b023} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015}. If SDNNF compiled to cSDD in quasi-polynomial time, then DNF would compile to cSDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore SDNNF cannot compile to cSDD in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_b13b0d78} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Wegener_2000}. \nedgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time (unless the polynomial hierarchy collapses) \citet{Darwiche_2002}. If SDNNF compiled to d-DNNF in polynomial time, then DNF would compile to d-DNNF in polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore SDNNF cannot compile to d-DNNF in polynomial time (unless the polynomial hierarchy collapses).

\edgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_b13b0d78} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Wegener_2000}. \nedgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_ea9b5299} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. If SDNNF compiled to d-SDNNF in quasi-polynomial time, then DNF would compile to d-SDNNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore SDNNF cannot compile to d-SDNNF in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_b13b0d78} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Wegener_2000}. \edgeref{lang_91f812d0}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}. \nedgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_ea9b5299} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. If SDNNF compiled to d-SDNNF$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time, then DNF would compile to d-SDNNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore SDNNF cannot compile to d-SDNNF$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_b13b0d78} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015,Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_4c204bf3} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Darwiche_2011}. If SDNNF compiled to DNF in quasi-polynomial time, then cSDD would compile to DNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore SDNNF cannot compile to DNF in quasi-polynomial time.

As defined by \citet{Amarilli_2018}, a structured \langref{DNNF} is a triple of the corresponding \langref{DNNF}, the v-tree $T$, and a mapping $\rho$; as such, extracting a \langref{DNNF} from a \langref{SDNNF} is trivial.

\edgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_b13b0d78} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_684b1ca7} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015}. If SDNNF compiled to FBDD in quasi-polynomial time, then d-SDNNF would compile to FBDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore SDNNF cannot compile to FBDD in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_b13b0d78} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015,Amarilli_2018}. \edgeref{lang_6ae90adc}{lang_4c204bf3} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_4c204bf3} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Darwiche_2011}. If SDNNF compiled to IP in quasi-polynomial time, then cSDD would compile to DNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore SDNNF cannot compile to IP in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_b13b0d78} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015,Amarilli_2018}. \edgeref{lang_e02902d0}{lang_89649e36} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_89649e36} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Darwiche_2011}. If SDNNF compiled to MODS in quasi-polynomial time, then cSDD would compile to CNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore SDNNF cannot compile to MODS in quasi-polynomial time.

First, we show no polynomial compilation exists.
\edgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_b13b0d78} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015}. If SDNNF compiled to nFBDD in polynomial time, then d-SDNNF would compile to nFBDD in polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore SDNNF cannot compile to nFBDD in polynomial time.

Now, we show a quasi-polynomial compilation exists.
\edgeref{lang_b13b0d78}{lang_d24efe0e} in at worst quasi-polynomial time \citet{Bollig_2018,Beame_2015}. \edgeref{lang_d24efe0e}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. Therefore SDNNF compiles to nFBDD in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_b13b0d78}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \edgeref{lang_3bebcab7}{lang_5bf00851} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. Therefore SDNNF compiles to NNF in polynomial time.
