If SDNNF compiled to nOBDD in polynomial time, then d-SDNNF would compile to nFBDD in polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore SDNNF cannot compile to nOBDD in polynomial time.

quasiDescription.description: Theorem 2 of \citet{Bollig_2018} shows that \langref{SDNNF} can be compiled into \langref{nOBDD} by adapting the compilation of \citet{Beame_2015} from \langref{DNNF} to \langref{nFBDD}.

matrix.description:
First, we show no polynomial compilation exists.
\edgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_b13b0d78} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \edgeref{lang_d24efe0e}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015}. If SDNNF compiled to nOBDD in polynomial time, then d-SDNNF would compile to nFBDD in polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore SDNNF cannot compile to nOBDD in polynomial time.

Now, we show a quasi-polynomial compilation exists.
Theorem 2 of \citet{Bollig_2018} shows that \langref{SDNNF} can be compiled into \langref{nOBDD} by adapting the compilation of \citet{Beame_2015} from \langref{DNNF} to \langref{nFBDD}.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_b13b0d78} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015,Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_d69995dd} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. If SDNNF compiled to OBDD$_<$ in quasi-polynomial time, then cSDD would compile to OBDD$_<$ in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore SDNNF cannot compile to OBDD$_<$ in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_b13b0d78} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015,Amarilli_2018}. \edgeref{lang_27fffab2}{lang_89649e36} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_89649e36} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Darwiche_2011}. If SDNNF compiled to PI in quasi-polynomial time, then cSDD would compile to CNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore SDNNF cannot compile to PI in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_b13b0d78} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Wegener_2000}. \edgeref{lang_1afefbe2}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015}. \nedgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_ea9b5299} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. If SDNNF compiled to SDD in quasi-polynomial time, then DNF would compile to d-SDNNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore SDNNF cannot compile to SDD in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_b13b0d78} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Wegener_2000}. \edgeref{lang_9c84a267}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2011,VanDenBroeck_2015}. \nedgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_ea9b5299} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. If SDNNF compiled to SDD$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time, then DNF would compile to d-SDNNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore SDNNF cannot compile to SDD$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_b13b0d78} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \edgeref{lang_4e62a038}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015}. If SDNNF compiled to uFBDD in polynomial time, then d-SDNNF would compile to nFBDD in polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore SDNNF cannot compile to uFBDD in polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_b13b0d78} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Wegener_2000}. \edgeref{lang_c2df8c2b}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_ea9b5299} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. If SDNNF compiled to uOBDD in quasi-polynomial time, then DNF would compile to d-SDNNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore SDNNF cannot compile to uOBDD in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_b13b0d78} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015,Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_b9d72a7c} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Bova_2016_a}. If SDNNF compiled to OBDD in quasi-polynomial time, then cSDD would compile to OBDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore SDNNF cannot compile to OBDD in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_b13b0d78} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \edgeref{lang_981b62f0}{lang_684b1ca7} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Beame_2013}. \nedgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_684b1ca7} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015}. If SDNNF compiled to dec-DNNF in quasi-polynomial time, then d-SDNNF would compile to FBDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore SDNNF cannot compile to dec-DNNF in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_b13b0d78} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015,Amarilli_2018}. \edgeref{lang_0f27d539}{lang_b9d72a7c} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015,Darwiche_2002}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_b9d72a7c} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Bova_2016_a}. If SDNNF compiled to dec-SDNNF in quasi-polynomial time, then cSDD would compile to OBDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore SDNNF cannot compile to dec-SDNNF in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_b13b0d78} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015,Amarilli_2018}. \edgeref{lang_4ae03bc8}{lang_b9d72a7c} in at worst quasi-polynomial time \citet{Oztok_2014,Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_b9d72a7c} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Bova_2016_a}. If SDNNF compiled to dec-SDNNF$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time, then cSDD would compile to OBDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore SDNNF cannot compile to dec-SDNNF$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_b13b0d78} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Wegener_2000}. \edgeref{lang_82fa749e}{lang_83e3b023} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2011}. \nedgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_83e3b023} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015}. If SDNNF compiled to cSDD$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time, then DNF would compile to cSDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore SDNNF cannot compile to cSDD$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_b13b0d78} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \edgeref{lang_e827cf31}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time \citet{Wegener_2000,Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015}. If SDNNF compiled to nOBDD$_<$ in polynomial time, then d-SDNNF would compile to nFBDD in polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore SDNNF cannot compile to nOBDD$_<$ in polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_b13b0d78} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Wegener_2000}. \edgeref{lang_43a33aec}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{Wegener_2000,Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_ea9b5299} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. If SDNNF compiled to uOBDD$_<$ in quasi-polynomial time, then DNF would compile to d-SDNNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore SDNNF cannot compile to uOBDD$_<$ in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_3c803ba1}{lang_b13b0d78} in polynomial time \citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}. \edgeref{lang_b13b0d78}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. Therefore SDNNF$_T$ compiles to DNNF in polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_3c803ba1}{lang_b13b0d78} in polynomial time \citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}. \edgeref{lang_b13b0d78}{lang_d24efe0e} in at worst quasi-polynomial time \citet{Bollig_2018,Beame_2015}. \edgeref{lang_d24efe0e}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. Therefore SDNNF$_T$ compiles to nFBDD in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_3c803ba1}{lang_b13b0d78} in polynomial time \citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}. \edgeref{lang_b13b0d78}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \edgeref{lang_3bebcab7}{lang_5bf00851} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. Therefore SDNNF$_T$ compiles to NNF in polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_3c803ba1}{lang_b13b0d78} in polynomial time \citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}. \edgeref{lang_b13b0d78}{lang_d24efe0e} in at worst quasi-polynomial time \citet{Bollig_2018,Beame_2015}. Therefore SDNNF$_T$ compiles to nOBDD in quasi-polynomial time.

\langref{SDNNF} is the union of the fixed-vtree families \langfam{SDNNF}{T}. Hence any \langfam{SDNNF}{T} formula is already an \langref{SDNNF} formula, so the compilation is trivial and polynomial-time.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_4e62a038} in at worst quasi-polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015,Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_89649e36} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Darwiche_2011}. If uFBDD compiled to CNF in quasi-polynomial time, then cSDD would compile to CNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore uFBDD cannot compile to CNF in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_4e62a038} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Beame_2013}. \nedgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_83e3b023} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Amarilli_2018,VanDenBroeck_2015}. If uFBDD compiled to cSDD in quasi-polynomial time, then d-DNNF would compile to cSDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore uFBDD cannot compile to cSDD in quasi-polynomial time.

Rewrite each decision node labeled $x$ as $(x \wedge D_0) \vee (\neg x \wedge D_1)$, where $D_0, D_1$ are the rewritings of the 0- and 1-successors. The $\wedge$-gates are decomposable, and unambiguity of the \langref{uFBDD} ensures all introduced $\vee$-gates are deterministic, yielding a \langref{d-DNNF} in linear time \citet{Amarilli_2018}.

\edgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_4e62a038} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Beame_2013}. \nedgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_ea9b5299} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Amarilli_2018}. If uFBDD compiled to d-SDNNF in quasi-polynomial time, then d-DNNF would compile to d-SDNNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore uFBDD cannot compile to d-SDNNF in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_4e62a038} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Beame_2013}. \edgeref{lang_91f812d0}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}. \nedgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_ea9b5299} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Amarilli_2018}. If uFBDD compiled to d-SDNNF$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time, then d-DNNF would compile to d-SDNNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore uFBDD cannot compile to d-SDNNF$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_4e62a038} in at worst quasi-polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015,Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_4c204bf3} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Darwiche_2011}. If uFBDD compiled to DNF in quasi-polynomial time, then cSDD would compile to DNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore uFBDD cannot compile to DNF in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_4e62a038}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \edgeref{lang_1df07cc3}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. Therefore uFBDD compiles to DNNF in polynomial time.

Consider the Boolean functions $\phi_n, \psi_n: \{0,1\}^{n^2}\to\{0,1\}$, which respectively test whether, in an $n\times n$ Boolean matrix, that the number of 1's is odd and there is a row full of 1's, and that the number of 1's is even and there is a column full of 1's. \citet{Bova_2016} and \citet{Wegener_2000} show that an \langref{FBDD} expression for $\phi_n \vee \psi_n$ has necessarily size $2^{\Omega(n^{1/2})}$; but since an \langref{FBDD} of size $O(n^2)$ may test $\phi_n$ or $\psi_n$ separately (shown by Proposition 3.1 of \citet{Amarilli_2018}), a \langref{uFBDD} for $\phi_n\vee \psi_n$ may be obtained by simply adding an $\vee$-gate joining these two \langref{FBDD} expressions, since $\phi_n \wedge \psi_n$ is unsatisfiable.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_4e62a038} in at worst quasi-polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015,Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015}. \edgeref{lang_6ae90adc}{lang_4c204bf3} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_4c204bf3} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Darwiche_2011}. If uFBDD compiled to IP in quasi-polynomial time, then cSDD would compile to DNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore uFBDD cannot compile to IP in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_4e62a038} in at worst quasi-polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015,Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015}. \edgeref{lang_e02902d0}{lang_89649e36} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_89649e36} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Darwiche_2011}. If uFBDD compiled to MODS in quasi-polynomial time, then cSDD would compile to CNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore uFBDD cannot compile to MODS in quasi-polynomial time.
As defined by \citet{Amarilli_2018}, \langref{uFBDD} is the subset of \langref{nFBDD} satisfying unambiguity.

\edgeref{lang_4e62a038}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \edgeref{lang_1df07cc3}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \edgeref{lang_3bebcab7}{lang_5bf00851} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. Therefore uFBDD compiles to NNF in polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_4e62a038} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Beame_2013}. \nedgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_d24efe0e} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Amarilli_2018}. If uFBDD compiled to nOBDD in quasi-polynomial time, then d-DNNF would compile to nOBDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore uFBDD cannot compile to nOBDD in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_4e62a038} in at worst quasi-polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015,Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_d69995dd} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. If uFBDD compiled to OBDD$_<$ in quasi-polynomial time, then cSDD would compile to OBDD$_<$ in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore uFBDD cannot compile to OBDD$_<$ in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_4e62a038} in at worst quasi-polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015,Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015}. \edgeref{lang_27fffab2}{lang_89649e36} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_89649e36} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Darwiche_2011}. If uFBDD compiled to PI in quasi-polynomial time, then cSDD would compile to CNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore uFBDD cannot compile to PI in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_4e62a038} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Beame_2013}. \edgeref{lang_1afefbe2}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015}. \nedgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_ea9b5299} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Amarilli_2018}. If uFBDD compiled to SDD in quasi-polynomial time, then d-DNNF would compile to d-SDNNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore uFBDD cannot compile to SDD in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_4e62a038} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Beame_2013}. \edgeref{lang_9c84a267}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2011,VanDenBroeck_2015}. \nedgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_ea9b5299} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Amarilli_2018}. If uFBDD compiled to SDD$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time, then d-DNNF would compile to d-SDNNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore uFBDD cannot compile to SDD$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_4e62a038} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Beame_2013}. \edgeref{lang_b13b0d78}{lang_d24efe0e} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015}. \nedgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_d24efe0e} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Amarilli_2018}. If uFBDD compiled to SDNNF in quasi-polynomial time, then d-DNNF would compile to nOBDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore uFBDD cannot compile to SDNNF in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_4e62a038} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Beame_2013}. \edgeref{lang_3c803ba1}{lang_d24efe0e} in at worst quasi-polynomial time \citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015}. \nedgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_d24efe0e} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Amarilli_2018}. If uFBDD compiled to SDNNF$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time, then d-DNNF would compile to nOBDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore uFBDD cannot compile to SDNNF$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_4e62a038} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Beame_2013}. \edgeref{lang_c2df8c2b}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_ea9b5299} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Amarilli_2018}. If uFBDD compiled to uOBDD in quasi-polynomial time, then d-DNNF would compile to d-SDNNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore uFBDD cannot compile to uOBDD in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_4e62a038} in at worst quasi-polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015,Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_b9d72a7c} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Bova_2016_a}. If uFBDD compiled to OBDD in quasi-polynomial time, then cSDD would compile to OBDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore uFBDD cannot compile to OBDD in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_4e62a038} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Beame_2013}. \edgeref{lang_981b62f0}{lang_684b1ca7} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Beame_2013}. \nedgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_684b1ca7} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2000}. If uFBDD compiled to dec-DNNF in quasi-polynomial time, then d-DNNF would compile to FBDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore uFBDD cannot compile to dec-DNNF in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_4e62a038} in at worst quasi-polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015,Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015}. \edgeref{lang_0f27d539}{lang_b9d72a7c} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015,Darwiche_2002}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_b9d72a7c} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Bova_2016_a}. If uFBDD compiled to dec-SDNNF in quasi-polynomial time, then cSDD would compile to OBDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore uFBDD cannot compile to dec-SDNNF in quasi-polynomial time.

\edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_4e62a038} in at worst quasi-polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015,Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015}. \edgeref{lang_4ae03bc8}{lang_b9d72a7c} in at worst quasi-polynomial time \citet{Oztok_2014,Amarilli_2018,Bollig_2018,Beame_2015}. \nedgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_b9d72a7c} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Bova_2016_a}. If uFBDD compiled to dec-SDNNF$_T$ in quasi-polynomial time, then cSDD would compile to OBDD in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. \edgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_4e62a038} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Beame_2013}. \edgeref{lang_82fa749e}{lang_83e3b023} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2011}. \nedgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_83e3b023} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,Amarilli_2018,VanDenBroeck_2015}. Construct a right-linear v-tree from the variable order, then rewrite each decision node labeled $x$ as $(x \wedge D_0) \vee (\neg x \wedge D_1)$, where $D_0, D_1$ are the rewritings of the 0- and 1-successors. quasiDescription.description: Corollary 3.2 of \citet{Beame_2013} show quasi-polynomial compilation from \langref{dec-DNNF} to \langref{FBDD}

matrix.description:
First, we show no polynomial compilation exists.
\edgeref{lang_0f27d539}{lang_981b62f0} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \edgeref{lang_684b1ca7}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_0f27d539}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Beame_2013}. Now, we show a quasi-polynomial compilation exists.
Corollary 3.2 of \citet{Beame_2013} show quasi-polynomial compilation from \langref{dec-DNNF} to \langref{FBDD}

\edgeref{lang_684b1ca7}{lang_981b62f0} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018}. \edgeref{lang_6ae90adc}{lang_4c204bf3} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \nedgeref{lang_684b1ca7}{lang_4c204bf3} in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Wegener_2000}. If dec-DNNF compiled to IP in quasi-polynomial time, then FBDD would compile to DNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore dec-DNNF cannot compile to IP in quasi-polynomial time.

If dec-DNNF compiled to MODS in quasi-polynomial time, then FBDD would compile to CNF in at most quasi-polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore dec-DNNF cannot compile to MODS in quasi-polynomial time.

If dec-DNNF compiled to nFBDD in polynomial time, then dec-SDNNF would compile to nFBDD in polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore dec-DNNF cannot compile to nFBDD in polynomial time.

quasiDescription.description: Theorem 2 of \citet{Bollig_2018} shows that \langref{SDNNF} can be compiled into \langref{nOBDD} by adapting the compilation of \citet{Beame_2015} from \langref{DNNF} to \langref{nFBDD}; proposition 2 of \citet{Bollig_2018} shows that this \langref{nOBDD} is unambiguous if the \langref{SDNNF} is deterministic. Additionally, the same compilation yields an \langref{OBDD} if the input is a \langref{dec-SDNNF} since there are no $\vee$-gates that are not decision gates in a \langref{dec-SDNNF}.

matrix.description:
First, we show no polynomial compilation exists.
\edgeref{lang_b9d72a7c}{lang_684b1ca7} in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}. \nedgeref{lang_0f27d539}{lang_684b1ca7} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Beame_2013}. Now, we show a quasi-polynomial compilation exists.
Theorem 2 of \citet{Bollig_2018} shows that \langref{SDNNF} can be compiled into \langref{nOBDD} by adapting the compilation of \citet{Beame_2015} from \langref{DNNF} to \langref{nFBDD}; proposition 2 of \citet{Bollig_2018} shows that this \langref{nOBDD} is unambiguous if the \langref{SDNNF} is deterministic. Additionally, the same compilation yields an \langref{OBDD} if the input is a \langref{dec-SDNNF} since there are no $\vee$-gates that are not decision gates in a \langref{dec-SDNNF}.
As defined by \citet{Amarilli_2018}, a \langref{dec-SDNNF} is simply a structured \langref{dec-DNNF}, so discarding the v-tree completes the compilation.

\edgeref{lang_e827cf31}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time \citet{Wegener_2000,Amarilli_2018}. \nedgeref{lang_0f27d539}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time \citet{Amarilli_2018,Beame_2013}. Therefore dec-SDNNF cannot compile to nOBDD$_<$ in polynomial time.

If dec-SDNNF compiled to uOBDD$_<$ in polynomial time, then dec-SDNNF would compile to nFBDD in polynomial time, contradicting the above. Therefore dec-SDNNF cannot compile to uOBDD$_<$ in polynomial time.

separatingFunctions.name: $\Sigma_n = \bigwedge_{i=0}^{n-1} \left( x_{2i} \vee x_{2i+1} \right)$
separatingFunctions.description: Requires that in each pair $(x_{2i}, x_{2i+1})$, at least one is true. Dual to the Pairwise DNF.

separatingFunctions.name: $\Pi_n = \bigvee_{i=0}^{n-1} \left( x_{2i} \wedge x_{2i+1} \right)$
separatingFunctions.description: Requires that at least one pair $(x_{2i}, x_{2i+1})$ has both variables true. Dual to the Pairwise CNF.

separatingFunctions.name: Parity function
separatingFunctions.description: $O_n = \bigoplus_{i=0}^{n-1} x_i$ has linear size $OBBD_<$ representations but exponential size CNF and DNF representations.

separatingFunctions.name: Hidden Weighted Bit
separatingFunctions.description: Let $w = \sum_{i=1}^n x_i$ be the Hamming weight of the input. Then $HWB_n(x_1, \ldots, x_n) = x_w$ (the $w$-th input bit). Has \langref{OBDD} size $2^{\Omega(n)}$ but polynomial \langref{SDD} and \langref{FBDD} size.

separatingFunctions.name: $OR_n = \bigvee_{i=1}^{n} x_i$
separatingFunctions.description: Separates languages that compactly represent disjunctions from those that cannot.

separatingFunctions.name: $\Sigma_n = \bigwedge_{i=1}^n (x_i \leftrightarrow y_i)$
separatingFunctions.description: Requires that each pair $(x_i, y_i)$ has equal truth values. An \langfam{OBDD}{<} with interleaved order $x_1 < y_1 < \cdots < x_n < y_n$ is polynomial, but a separated order $x_1 < \cdots < x_n < y_1 < \cdots < y_n$ forces exponential size.

separatingFunctions.name: Clique Detection Instances
separatingFunctions.description: Tests whether a directed graph on $n$ vertices (encoded as $I \in \{0,1\}^{n^2}$) contains a bidirectional clique of size $k$: $\Sigma_{n,k}(I) = 1 \iff I$ contains such a clique. Has $O(n^3)$ prime implicants but exponential \langref{FBDD} size for suitable $k$.

separatingFunctions.name: Sauerhoff function
separatingFunctions.description: Let $g_n: \{0,1\}^{2n} \to \{0,1\}$ be the function evaluating to 1 if and only if the sum of its inputs is divisible by 3. The Sauerhoff function $S_n: \{0,1\}^{n^2} \to \{0,1\}$ is defined on the $n \times n$ matrix $X = (x_{i,j})$ as $S_n(X) = R_n(X) \vee C_n(X)$, where $R_n, C_n : \{0,1\}^{n^2} \to \{0,1\}$ are defined by $R_n(X) = \bigplus_{i=1}^n g_n(x_{i1},x_{i2},...,x_{in}$ and $C_n(X) = R_n(X^T)$.

separatingFunctions.name: Jukna-Schnitger function
separatingFunctions.description: Tests triangle-freeness on graphs with respect to a fixed set of forbidden triangles $A \subset T_n$. \citet{Jukna_2002} show that for every $n$, there exists $A_n$ of size $O(n^2)$ such that any balanced rectangle cover of $JS_n^{A_n}$ has size $2^{\Omega(n^2)}$. Has \langref{PI} size $O(n^2)$ but \langref{DNNF} size $2^{\Omega(n^2)}$.

separatingFunctions.name: Generalized Hidden Weighted Bit
separatingFunctions.description: A variant of $HWB_n$ introduced by \citet{Bova_2016_a} on $2n+1$ variables $x_1, \ldots, x_n, y_0, \ldots, y_n$. Defined as $F_n = (P_0 \wedge \neg y_0) \vee (P_n \wedge y_n) \vee \bigvee_{i=1}^{n-1}((P_{i,0} \wedge \neg y_i) \vee (P_{i,1} \wedge y_i))$, where $P_j$ and $P_{i,b}$ are the partition functions from the HWB SDD construction. The $y_i$ variables ensure all sub-SDDs are pairwise non-equivalent, making the SDD compressed. Has \langref{cSDD} size $O(n^3)$ but \langref{OBDD} size $2^{\Omega(n)}$, since conditioning on $y_i = 1$ recovers $HWB_n$.

definitions.title: Boolean Formula
definitions.statement:
Fix a countable set $X = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots\}$ of propositional variables \citep{Boole_1847}.

A \emph{Boolean formula} is a syntactic expression built inductively from variables $x_i \in X$, constants $\top$ and $\bot$, and connectives $\neg, \wedge, \vee$ in the usual way.

Each formula $\alpha$ of arity $n$ over variables $\vec{x} = (x_1, x_2, \cdots, x_n)$ denotes a Boolean function $\alpha : \{0,1\}^n \to \{0,1\}$. Two formulas are \emph{semantically equivalent}, written $\alpha \equiv \beta$, when they agree on every truth assignment ($\alpha(\vec{x}) = \beta(\vec{x}), \forall \vec{x} \in \{0,1\}^n$). For the purposes of this project we only consider formulas with finite arity.

definitions.title: Representation Language
definitions.statement:
A \emph{representation language} $L$ is a set of Boolean formulas sharing a common syntactic structure, endowed with the standard Boolean semantics and a size measure $|\cdot|$. (TODO: Consider more formally defining the size measure or use a rigorous definition?)

\citet{Darwiche_2002} distinguish a representation language from a \emph{target compilation language}: the former is intended to be natural for humans to read and write (e.g. CNF), while the latter need not be human-intelligible but must support a non-trivial class of polytime queries and transformations.

For a language to qualify as a target compilation language, \citet{Darwiche_2002} require at minimum that clausal entailment is decidable in polynomial time; polytime consistency alone does not suffice to justify the cost of off-line compilation.

definitions.title: Language Family
definitions.statement:
We also introduce a notion of Language Families. A \emph{language family} is a parameterized collection $\mathcal{F} = \{L_\theta\}_{\theta \in \Theta}$ of representation languages.
For example, consider the \emph{family} \langfam{OBDD}{<} parameterized by total variable orderings $<$ on $X$, and the \emph{language} \langref{OBDD} $= \bigcup_< \langfam{OBDD}{<}$. One of the main contributions of this paper is to rigorously differentiate the results between language families and their corresponding languages, results on which are sometimes vastly different.

definitions.title: Succinctness Between Languages
definitions.statement:
There are two intuitive definitions of succinctness: a \emph{time} requirement, i.e. that an efficient compilation algorithm exists between two languages; or the \emph{space} requirement, that formulas remain ``small'' in the new language. Since, for any computation, runtime is lower-bounded by output size, the latter definition is weaker.

We fix the convention that succinctness between representation languages is a size-only relation, with no requirement that a conversion algorithm exist \citep{Gogic_1995}. However, to the best of our knowledge, all known proofs of succinctness use a compilation algorithm to establish the size relationship; and all proofs that \emph{no} efficient compilation algorithm exists use size bounds. An example to the contrary would be highly interesting. (TODO: Deeply search to make sure this truly holds)

Formally, let $L_1$ and $L_2$ be two languages. $L_1$ is \emph{at least as succinct as} $L_2$, written $L_1 \leq L_2$, if there exists a polynomial $p$ such that for every $\alpha \in L_2$ there exists an equivalent $\beta \in L_1$ with $|\beta| \leq p(|\alpha|)$.

We say $L_1$ \emph{polynomially compiles into} $L_2$, and write $L_1 \leq_p L_2$, if there exists a polynomial-time algorithm that converts any $\alpha \in L_1$ into an equivalent $\beta \in L_2$; again, for the purposes of this project, the two definitions are functionally equivalent.

We additionally define quasi-polynomial succinctness identically, except we require a quasi-polynomial function $q(n) \in n^{log^{O(1)}(n)}$ satisfying the above properties and use the notation $\leq_q$.

definitions.title: Succinctness Between Families
definitions.statement:
Let $\mathcal{F}_1$ and $\mathcal{F}_2$ be language families. We extend the language-level succinctness definition of \citet{Gogic_1995} to families by writing: $\mathcal{F}_1$ is \emph{at least as succinct as} $\mathcal{F}_2$, written $\mathcal{F}_1 \leq \mathcal{F}_2$, if for every $L_2 \in \mathcal{F}_2$ there exists $L_1 \in \mathcal{F}_1$ so that $L_1 \leq L_2$.

Intuitively, this forces all languages in the ``source'' family ($\mathcal{F}_2$) to efficiently compile into at least one member of the ``target'' ($\mathcal{F}_1$).

As with the language-level definition, succinctness between families is purely existential with respect to size and places no requirement on algorithmic time complexity. We also extend this definition to define a succinctness relationship between an individual language $L$ and a family $\mathcal{F}$ by applying the above definition to the singleton $\{L\}$ and $\mathcal{F}$.

definitions.title: Tractability of an Operation
definitions.statement: An operation $\mathcal{O}$ is \emph{tractable} on a representation language $L$ if there exists a polynomial-time (resp. quasi-polynomial-time) algorithm that performs $\mathcal{O}$ on any input formula in $L$. Following \citet{Selman_1996}: a propositional theory is compiled off-line into a target language $L$, after which all subsequent on-line queries are answered efficiently \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

definitions.title: Query Operation
definitions.statement:
A \emph{query operation} takes a formula $\alpha \in L$ (and possibly auxiliary inputs) and returns a Boolean answer or a numerical value without modifying $\alpha$. We consider the query operations below, as laid out in \citet{Darwiche_2002}.
\begin{itemize}[noitemsep]
  \item \emph{Consistency} (CO): is $\alpha$ satisfiable? (i.e. is there an $\vec{x}$ so that $\alpha(\vec{x}) = \top$?)
  \item \emph{Validity} (VA): is $\alpha \equiv \top$?
  \item \emph{Clausal entailment} (CE): given a clause $c$, does $\alpha \models c$? (i.e. is $\alpha \wedge \neg c$ unsatisfiable?)
  \item \emph{Implicant} (IM): given a term $t$, does $t \models \alpha$? (i.e. is $t \wedge \neg \alpha$ unsatisfiable?)
  \item \emph{Equivalence} (EQ): is $\alpha \equiv \beta$ for a given $\beta$? (i.e. does $\alpha(\vec{x}) = \beta(\vec{x})$ for all $\vec{x}$?)
  \item \emph{Model counting} (CT): compute $|\{ \vec{x} : \alpha(\vec{x}) = \top \}|$.
  \item \emph{Model enumeration} (ME): enumerate all satisfying assignments (i.e. return $\{ \vec{x} : \alpha(\vec{x}) = \top \}$).
  \item \emph{Sentential entailment} (SE): does $\alpha \models \beta$ for a given sentence $\beta$?
\end{itemize}

definitions.title: Transformation Operation
definitions.statement:
A \emph{transformation operation} maps one or more formulas in a language $L$ to a new formula. We require the output formula to be in $L$. The canonical transformations cataloged by \citet{Darwiche_2002} are:
\begin{itemize}[noitemsep]
  \item \emph{Conditioning} (CD): given a partial assignment $\gamma$, compute $\alpha|_{\gamma}$ (i.e. ``plug in'' the assignment $\gamma$).
  \item \emph{Singleton Forgetting} (SFO): given a variable $x_i$, compute $(\alpha|_{x_i=0}) \vee (\alpha|_{x_i=1})$.
  \item \emph{Forgetting} (FO): given a \emph{subset} of variables $\gamma = \{x_{i_1}, x_{i_2}, \ldots, x_{i_k}\} \subseteq X$, compute $\bigvee_{\gamma \in \{0,1\}^k} \alpha \mid \gamma$.
  \item \emph{Conjunction} ($\wedge$C): compute $\alpha \wedge \beta$.
  \item \emph{Disjunction} ($\vee$C): compute $\alpha \vee \beta$.

(TODO: Differentiate between bounded and unbounded conjunction/disjunction)
\end{itemize}
Unlike query operations, transformations must produce new representable elements; tractability therefore requires both efficient execution \emph{and} that the output remains in $L$.

(TODO: double check that these are the full set to include)

operationLemmas.description: CO and CD imply ME: First test consistency (polytime via CO). If inconsistent, return the empty set. Otherwise, build a decision tree of models by iterating over variables $x_1, \ldots, x_n$ and using CD to test consistency of $\Sigma | \alpha \land x_i$ and $\Sigma | \alpha \land \neg x_i$ for each leaf term $\alpha$. Since one branch must succeed for consistent terms, the tree has $O(mn)$ tests where $m$ is the model count and $n$ is the variable count.
CO and CD imply CE: To test whether sentence $\Sigma$ entails non-valid clause $\alpha$, i.e. $\Sigma \models \alpha$, it suffices to test whether $\Sigma | \neg\alpha$ is inconsistent.

VA and CD imply IM: To test whether consistent term $\alpha$ entails sentence $\Sigma$, i.e. $\alpha \models \Sigma$, test whether $
eg\alpha \lor \Sigma$ is valid. This is equivalent to $
eg\alpha \lor (\Sigma | \alpha)$. Since $
eg\alpha$ and $\Sigma | \alpha$ share no variables, the disjunction is valid iff $
eg\alpha$ is valid or $\Sigma | \alpha$ is valid. Since $\alpha$ is consistent, $
eg\alpha$ cannot be valid. Thus we test validity of $\Sigma | \alpha$, constructible in polytime via CD and testable in polytime via VA.
EQ implies CO: $\mathsf{true}$ and $\mathsf{false}$ belong to every NNF subset. $\Sigma$ is inconsistent iff it is equivalent to $\mathsf{false}$.
EQ implies VA: $\mathsf{true}$ and $\mathsf{false}$ belong to every NNF subset. $\Sigma$ is valid iff it is equivalent to $\mathsf{true}$.
SE implies EQ: Sentences $\Sigma_1$ and $\Sigma_2$ are equivalent iff $\Sigma_1 \models \Sigma_2$ and $\Sigma_2 \models \Sigma_1$.
CT implies CO: Model counting (CT) requires enumerating all satisfying assignments. If a formula is inconsistent, it has zero models. Thus CT can determine CO by checking if the model count is zero.
CT implies VA: A formula is valid iff all $2^n$ assignments satisfy it. If we can count models in polytime (CT), we can check validity by testing if the count equals $2^n$.

IM implies VA: A formula $\Sigma$ is valid iff the complete term (all positive or all negative literals, i.e., any single model) implies $\Sigma$. Since IM tests whether a consistent term implies the sentence, testing with a trivially consistent term gives validity.
CE implies CO: A formula $\Sigma$ is inconsistent iff $\Sigma \models \bot$, which is equivalent to $\Sigma$ entailing the empty clause. Clause entailment (CE) can test this directly.

ME implies CO: Model Enumeration requires outputting all models in time polynomial in the input size and number of models. In particular, it must determine whether any model exists (output the first model or halt with the empty set) in polynomial time, which is exactly the Consistency (CO) query.

Conjunction implies bounded conjunction: bounded conjunction is a special case of conjunction where one operand has bounded size, so any language closed under conjunction is also closed under bounded conjunction.

Disjunction implies bounded disjunction: bounded disjunction is a special case of disjunction where one operand has bounded size, so any language closed under disjunction is also closed under bounded disjunction.
Forgetting implies singleton forgetting: singleton forgetting eliminates a single variable, which is a special case of forgetting an arbitrary set of variables.

Conditioning and bounded disjunction imply singleton forgetting: to forget variable $x$ from $\Sigma$, compute $\Sigma|_{x=0}$ and $\Sigma|_{x=1}$ via conditioning, then combine via $\Sigma|_{x=0} \lor \Sigma|_{x=1}$ using bounded disjunction (one operand can serve as the bounded component).
Negation and conjunction imply disjunction (De Morgan): $\Sigma_1 \lor \Sigma_2 = \lnot(\lnot\Sigma_1 \land \lnot\Sigma_2)$. Negate both operands, conjoin, then negate the result.
Negation and disjunction imply conjunction (De Morgan): $\Sigma_1 \land \Sigma_2 = \lnot(\lnot\Sigma_1 \lor \lnot\Sigma_2)$. Negate both operands, disjoin, then negate the result.

Negation and bounded conjunction imply bounded disjunction (De Morgan): $\Sigma \lor \gamma = \lnot(\lnot\Sigma \land \lnot\gamma)$, where $\lnot\gamma$ (negation of a clause) is a term. Negate $\Sigma$, conjoin with the term $\lnot\gamma$ via bounded conjunction, then negate.

Negation and bounded disjunction imply bounded conjunction (De Morgan): $\Sigma \land \alpha = \lnot(\lnot\Sigma \lor \lnot\alpha)$, where $\lnot\alpha$ (negation of a term) is a clause. Negate $\Sigma$, disjoin with the clause $\lnot\alpha$ via bounded disjunction, then negate.
Negation and consistency imply validity: $\Sigma$ is valid iff $\lnot\Sigma$ is inconsistent. Negate via $\lnot$C, then test consistency via CO.
Negation and validity imply consistency: $\Sigma$ is consistent iff $\lnot\Sigma$ is not valid. Negate via $\lnot$C, then test validity via VA.

Negation and clausal entailment imply implicant testing: term $\alpha$ implies $\Sigma$ iff $\lnot\Sigma$ entails clause $\lnot\alpha$. Negate $\Sigma$ via $\lnot$C, then test clausal entailment of $\lnot\alpha$ (a clause) via CE.

Negation and implicant testing imply clausal entailment: $\Sigma$ entails clause $\gamma$ iff term $\lnot\gamma$ implies $\lnot\Sigma$. Negate $\Sigma$ via $\lnot$C, then test whether $\lnot\gamma$ (a term) implies $\lnot\Sigma$ via IM.

Negation, bounded conjunction, and consistency imply sentential entailment: $\Sigma_1 \models \Sigma_2$ iff $\Sigma_1 \land \lnot\Sigma_2$ is unsatisfiable. Negate $\Sigma_2$ via $\lnot$C, conjoin with $\Sigma_1$ via $\land$BC, then test consistency via CO.

Bounded conjunction and singleton forgetting imply conditioning: $\Sigma|\gamma$ for term $\gamma = l_1 \land \cdots \land l_k$ is computed iteratively by $\Sigma \leftarrow \exists x_i.(\Sigma \land l_i)$ for each literal $l_i$, using $\land$BC then SFO.

CT and $\land$BC imply SE: $\Sigma_1 \models \Sigma_2$ iff $|\text{models}(\Sigma_1 \land \Sigma_2)| = |\text{models}(\Sigma_1)|$. Compute the conjunction via $\land$BC, then count models of both the original formula and the conjunction via CT and compare.
